blocks:
Ayahs

الٓمٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (١)
Alif, Lam, Mim, Ra. These are the signs of the Book. That which has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, but most people do not believe [in it]. (1)
ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى رَفَعَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ يُدَبِّرُ ٱلْأَمْرَ يُفَصِّلُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُم بِلِقَآءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ (٢)
It is Allah who raised the heavens without any pillars that you see, and then presided over the Throne. He disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. He directs the command, [and] elaborates the signs that you may be certain of encountering your Lord. (2)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَدَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنْهَـٰرًا ۖ وَمِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ جَعَلَ فِيهَا زَوْجَيْنِ ٱثْنَيْنِ ۖ يُغْشِى ٱلَّيْلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٣)
It is He who has spread out the earth and set in it firm mountains and streams, and of every fruit in it He has made two kinds. He draws the night’s cover over the day. There are indeed signs in that for a people who reflect. (3)
وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُّتَجَـٰوِرَٰتٌ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ مِّنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَزَرْعٌ وَنَخِيلٌ صِنْوَانٌ وَغَيْرُ صِنْوَانٍ يُسْقَىٰ بِمَآءٍ وَٰحِدٍ وَنُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَهَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فِى ٱلْأُكُلِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٤)
In the earth are neighbouring terrains [of diverse kinds] and vineyards, farms, and date palms growing from the same root and from diverse roots, [all] irrigated by the same water, and We give some of them an advantage over others in flavour. There are indeed signs in that for people who exercise their reason. (4)

Commentary

Signs of Allah in the Heavens, the Earth, and the World of Plants

Once again, we encounter the "Disjointed Letters" (Huruful-Muqatta'ah) of the Quran, which appear in 29 Surahs, at the beginning of this Surah. However, the letters mentioned here are actually a combination of "Alif, Lam, Mim" (الم), which appears at the beginning of several Surahs, and "Alif, Lam, Ra" (الر), which is stated at the start of others. In fact, this is the only Surah that begins with "Alif, Lam, Mim, Ra" (المر). Since it appears that the Disjointed Letters at the beginning of each Surah have a direct link to the content of that Surah, it is probable that this combination at the start of Surah Ar-Ra'd indicates that its content encompasses the themes of both groups of Surahs—those beginning with "Alm" and those beginning with "Alr." Interestingly, a careful study of the content of these Surahs confirms this point.

Regarding the interpretation of the Disjointed Letters of the Quran, we have already had detailed discussions at the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf, and we see no need to repeat them.

In any case, the first verse of this Surah speaks of the greatness of the Quran:

"These are the verses of the great Heavenly Book" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ) [^1] "and that which has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth" (وَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ الْحَقُّ).

There is no room for any doubt or hesitation regarding it, for it expresses the objective realities of the world of creation and its relationship with human beings.

It is a truth that is not mixed with falsehood; for this reason, the signs of its truthfulness are evident from its very face and require no further argumentation.

"But despite all this, the whimsical and ignorant people, who constitute the majority, do not believe in these verses" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ).

This is because if a human being is left to his own devices and does not accept the following of a pure-hearted teacher to guide and educate him on the path of life, and remains free to follow his whims, he will often lose his way and go astray.

However, if Divine educators and guides of the path of truth are their Imams and leaders, and the person places himself at their disposal, the majority will follow the path of truth.

Then, it proceeds to explain an important part of the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the signs of Allah in the world of creation. It takes the earthly human on a journey through the expanse of the heavens, showing him these magnificent celestial bodies, their systems, movements, and secrets, so that he may realize His infinite power and boundless wisdom. How beautifully He says:

[^1]: As we have said in the past, the choice of the distant demonstrative pronoun (تلك - those) instead of the near demonstrative pronoun (هذه - these) is an allusion to the greatness of the verses of the Quran and their high and exalted status.

"Allah is He who raised the heavens without pillars that you can see" or "He raised them with invisible pillars" (اللَّهُ الَّذِي رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها). [^1] Two interpretations have been given for the phrase "بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها": First, "As you see, the heaven is without pillars" (as if the original structure was: you see them without pillars). Second, that "you see them" (ترونها) is an adjective for "pillars" (عمد), meaning: He raised the heavens without pillars that are visible, which implies the existence of pillars for the heaven, but invisible ones!

This is exactly what has been narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام in the hadith of Husayn ibn Khalid. He says: I asked Imam Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه السلام about the meaning of Allah's words: "By the heaven full of paths" (وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ). He said: "This heaven has paths toward the earth..." Husayn ibn Khalid says: I asked, "How can it have paths of connection to the earth while Allah says the heavens are without pillars?" The Imam replied:

"Subhan Allah! Does Allah not say: 'without pillars that you can see' (بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها)?" I said, "Yes." He said: "Then there are pillars, but you do not see them." [^2]

[^1]: 'Amad (with the vowel on sad) and 'Umud (with the vowel on dal) are both plurals of 'Amud, meaning pillar. Although linguistically the first is considered a plural and the second a collective noun (Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse).

[^2]: This hadith is quoted in Tafsir al-Burhan from Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim and also from Tafsir al-Ayyashi (Al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 278).

This verse, in light of the hadith narrated regarding its interpretation, unveils a scientific reality that was not apparent to anyone at the time of the revelation of the verses. At that time, the Ptolemaic system ruled the scientific circles of the world and the thoughts of the people with absolute authority. According to it, the heavens were nested spheres like the layers of an onion, and naturally, none were suspended or without pillars; rather, each rested upon the other. However, about a thousand years after the revelation of these verses, human knowledge reached the point where the "onion-skin" celestial spheres were proven to be entirely imaginary. The reality is that the celestial bodies are each suspended and fixed in their own orbits and positions without having any physical support. The only thing that keeps them fixed in their places is the balance between the force of gravity and the centrifugal force—one related to the mass of these bodies and the other to their motion.

This balance of gravity and repulsion acts as an invisible pillar, keeping the celestial bodies in their places.

The hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in this regard is very interesting. According to this hadith, the Imam said:

"These stars that are in the sky are cities like the cities on earth; every city is connected to another (every star to another star) by a pillar of light." [^1]

Could a clearer or more expressive term than "invisible pillar" or "pillar of light" be found in the literary horizon of that day to describe gravitational waves and their balance with repulsive force? [^1] Then He says: "After creating these pillarless heavens, which are a clear sign of His greatness and infinite power, Allah established Himself upon the Throne, meaning He took hold of the governance of the universe" (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ). The meaning of "the Throne" (Arsh) and Allah's dominion over it was discussed sufficiently in Surah Al-A'raf under verse 54. [^2] After mentioning the creation of the heavens and the Lord's governance over them, He speaks of the subjugation of the sun and the moon: "He is the One who subjected the sun and the moon [to His law]" (وَ سَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ).

What subjugation could be greater than this—that all of them are subservient to His command and are servants to humans and all living beings? They radiate light, illuminate a world, keep the habitat of creatures warm, nurture living beings, create tides in the seas, and in short, are the source of all movements and blessings.

However, this system of the material world is not eternal or everlasting, "and each of them—the sun and the moon—continues to move in its course until a specified term appointed for them" (كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

Following that, He adds that these movements, rotations, comings and goings, and changes are not without calculation or account, nor are they without result or benefit; rather, "It is He who regulates all affairs," and for every movement, He has considered an account, and for every account, a goal (يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ).

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 574, quoting Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim Qummi.

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "The Quran and the Last Prophet," page 166 onwards.

[^2]: Refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 204.

"He details His signs for you and explains their intricacies so that you may have certainty in the meeting with your Lord and the Hereafter" (يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ).

Following the previous verse, which takes man to the heavens and shows the Divine signs in the upper world, the second verse among the monotheistic verses of this Surah invites man to study the earth, the mountains, the rivers, the varieties of fruits, and the rising and setting of the sun, so that he may reflect on what his place of rest and comfort was in the beginning and how it came to be this way. He says: "And He is the One who spread out the earth" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ).

He spread it out in such a way that it would be ready for human life and the cultivation of plants and animals. He filled the pits and steep, dangerous slopes by the erosion of mountains and the transformation of rocks into soil, making them flat and habitable, whereas the initial folds [of the earth] were such that they did not allow for human life.

There is also the possibility in this sentence that "spreading the earth" (مَدَّ الْأَرْضَ) refers to what geologists say: that the entire earth was initially covered under water, then the waters settled into basins and the dry lands gradually emerged from the water and expanded day by day until they reached their current form.

After that, He refers to the emergence of mountains and says: "Allah placed mountains in the earth" (وَ جَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ).

The same mountains that are introduced in other verses of the Quran as "pegs" (اوتاد) of the earth, perhaps because the mountains are gripped together from beneath and cover the entire surface of the earth like armor, neutralizing internal pressures from within and the extraordinary force of the moon's gravity and tides from without. In this way, they eliminate constant instability, agitation, and earthquakes, keeping the planet Earth in tranquility for human life.

The mention of mountains after the mention of the spreading of the earth seemingly indicates that the earth is not so flat that there are no heights or depths—in which case rains and waters would never settle on it, or everywhere would turn into a swamp and storms would constantly sweep across its surface—but with the creation of mountains, it became safe and secure from both aspects.

Nor is it all mountains and valleys such that it would be uninhabitable; rather, as a whole, it is a combination of flat, smooth areas and mountains and valleys, forming the best composition for the life of humans and other living creatures. Then, referring to the waters and rivers that flow upon the earth, He says: "And He placed rivers therein" (وَ أَنْهاراً).

The system of irrigating the earth via mountains, and the connection of mountains with rivers, is very fascinating. Many mountains on earth store water in the form of snow on their peaks or in the crevices of their valleys, which gradually melts and, by the law of gravity, flows from higher regions toward low and wide areas. Without the need for any other force, they naturally irrigate and saturate many lands throughout the year.

If it were not for the gentle slope of the lands, and if the waters were not stored in the mountains in this way, the irrigation of most arid regions would not be possible, or if possible, it would require an extraordinary cost.

After that, He proceeds to mention the food substances and fruits that come into existence from the earth, water, and sunlight, which are the best means for human nutrition, saying:

"And of all fruits, He placed therein two pairs" (وَ مِنْ كُلِّ الثَّمَراتِ جَعَلَ فِيها زَوْجَيْنِ اثْنَيْنِ). This is an allusion to the fact that fruits are living entities that possess male and female seeds, which become fertile through pollination.

If Linnaeus, the famous Swedish scientist and botanist, succeeded in the mid-18th century AD in discovering that duality (gender) in the plant world is almost a universal and general law, and that plants, like animals, become fertile and bear fruit through the union of male and female seeds, the Holy Quran revealed this truth 1,100 years before him. This is itself one of the scientific miracles of the Holy Quran, which expresses the greatness of this great Heavenly Book.

There is no doubt that before Linnaeus, many scientists had realized the existence of male and female in some plants in a general sense; even ordinary people knew, for example, that if the date palm is not "pollinated"—meaning if the male seed is not sprinkled on the female parts of the plant—it will not bear fruit. But no one knew for certain that this was an almost universal law until Linnaeus succeeded in discovering it. However, as we said, the Quran had unveiled it centuries before him.

And since the life of humans and all living beings, especially plants and fruits, is not possible without a precise system of day and night, He speaks of this subject in another part of this verse, saying: "Allah covers the day with the night and casts a veil over it" (يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ).

For if there were not the dark, soothing veil of the night, the continuous light of the sun would burn all plants, and no trace of fruits or living beings in general would remain on the face of the earth.

Even though on the moon the day is not permanent, the fact that the length of its days is equal to fifteen Earth days causes the temperature in the middle of the lunar day to rise so high that if there were water or any other liquid there, it would reach the boiling point and beyond. No living creature we know on Earth has the capacity to endure that heat under normal conditions.

At the end of the verse, He says: "In these subjects mentioned, there are signs for those who reflect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ).

Those who reflect on this marvelous and wondrous system—on the system of light and darkness, on the system of celestial bodies and their rotation, on the system of the sun and moon's radiance and their service to humanity, and on the system of the earth's expansion and the secrets of the emergence of mountains, rivers, plants, and fruits—yes, they clearly see in these signs the eternal power and infinite wisdom of the Creator.

In the last verse under discussion, He points to a series of interesting geological and botanical points, each of which is a sign of the calculated system of creation. First, He says: "And in the earth are diverse tracts, side by side and in each other's neighborhood" (وَ فِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ). [^1] Despite the fact that these tracts are all connected and related to one another, each has its own specific structure and potential. Some are firm, some soft, some salty, some sweet, and each has the potential to nurture a specific type of plant, fruit tree, or crop. This is because the needs of humans and earthly creatures are very vast and varied; it is as if each tract of land is commissioned to fulfill one of these needs. If all were uniform, or if the potentials were not correctly distributed among the tracts of land, what deficiencies would man face in terms of food, medicine, and other requirements? But with the calculated distribution of this mission and the granting of the potential to nurture it to different tracts of land, all these needs are fully met.

Another point is that "in this same earth there are gardens and trees of various grapes, crops, and date palms" (وَ جَنَّاتٌ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَ زَرْعٌ وَ نَخِيلٌ). [^1] And strangely, these trees and their various types "sometimes grow from a single base and trunk, and sometimes from different bases" (صِنْوانٌ وَ غَيْرُ صِنْوانٍ). [^2] Sinwan is the plural of sinw, which originally means a branch that comes out of the main trunk of a tree; therefore, sinwan means different branches that come out of one trunk.

Interestingly, it sometimes happens that each of these branches yields a specific type of fruit. This sentence might be an allusion to the potential of trees for grafting, where sometimes several different grafts are made on one base, and each of these grafts grows and yields a specific type of fruit to us. The soil is one, and the root and trunk are one, but its fruit and product are different and varied! And even more strangely, "all of them are watered with one water" (يُسْقى‌ بِماءٍ واحِدٍ).

And despite all this, "We make some of these trees excel over others in terms of fruit" (وَ نُفَضِّلُ بَعْضَها عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ فِي الْأُكُلِ).

We have even frequently seen that on one tree or one branch, there are fruits of the same kind that have different tastes and colors. In the world of flowers, it is often seen that one flower bush, and even one branch, presents flowers of completely different colors.

What mysterious laboratory has been placed within the branches of trees that produces completely different compounds from completely identical materials, each of which satisfies a part of human needs?

Is not each of these secrets a proof of the existence of a Wise and All-Knowing Origin who leads this system?

It is here that at the end of the verse He says: "In these matters are signs of Allah's greatness for those who exercise their intellect and think" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ).

[^1]: Mutajawir is from the root jar, meaning neighbor and near. But when He says "diverse tracts side by side" (قطع متجاورات), the concept is that they are different pieces placed next to each other; otherwise, if they were all identical, the expression "neighboring pieces" would have no meaning.

[^1]: A'nab is the plural of 'inab (grape) and nakhil is the plural of nakhl (date palm). Perhaps the use of the plural form here is an allusion to the various types of grapes and dates, as each of these two fruits may have hundreds of types in their own world with different properties, tastes, and colors.

[^2]: Another meaning has been mentioned for sinw, which is "similar," but it seems that this meaning is derived from the first meaning mentioned above.

Key Points

1- In the first verse under discussion,

At the beginning, reference was made to the secrets of creation and Monotheism (Tawhid), but at the end of the verse we read: "Allah explains His signs for you so that you may believe in the Resurrection and the Hereafter" (يُفَصِّلُ الْآياتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّكُمْ تُوقِنُونَ). The question arises: what is the relationship between the issue of Monotheism and the Hereafter, such that one is mentioned as the result of the other?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting that, firstly, Allah's power to create this world is a proof of His power to restore it, just as we read in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 29: "As He began you, so shall you return" (كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ). Or as we read at the end of Surah Ya-Sin: "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the like of them?"

Secondly, as we have said in discussions on the Hereafter, if the world of the Hereafter did not exist, the creation of this world would be futile, for this life alone cannot be the goal for the creation of this vast universe. The Holy Quran, among the verses related to the Hereafter (Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verse 62), says: "And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not take heed?" (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى‌ فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ). [^1]

2- Scientific Miracles of the Quran.

In the Holy Quran, there are numerous verses that unveil a series of scientific secrets that were hidden from the eyes of scientists at that time. This is itself a sign of the miracle and greatness of the Quran. Researchers who have discussed the miracle of the Quran have mostly referred to a portion of these verses.

One of these verses is the one mentioned above which discusses the duality (gender) of plants. As we said, the issue of duality in the plant world was known to man since ancient times as a partial case, but as a general and universal law, it was unveiled for the first time in Europe in the mid-18th century by the Italian scientist Linnaeus. However, the Quran had informed Muslims of it a thousand years earlier, even more.

This subject also appears in Surah Luqman, verse 10: "And We sent down water from the sky and caused to grow therein every kind of noble pair" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ).

Reference has been made to this subject in some other verses as well.

3- Subjugation of the Sun and the Moon.

In the above verses, we read that Allah has subjected the sun and the moon. We have numerous verses in the Holy Quran stating that the celestial bodies, earthly creatures, night and day, and the like, are all subjected to man. In one instance we read: "Allah subjected the rivers for you" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْأَنْهارَ - Ibrahim: 32).

In another instance He says: "He subjected the ships for you" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ - Ibrahim: 32). And elsewhere: "He subjected the night and the day for you" (سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ - An-Nahl: 12).

And elsewhere: "He subjected the sun and the moon for you" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ - Ibrahim: 33). And elsewhere: "He subjected the sea to you so that you may eat fresh meat from it" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا - An-Nahl: 14).

And elsewhere He says: "Do you not see that Allah has subjected to you all that is on the earth?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ - Al-Hajj: 65).

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to the book "Resurrection and the World After Death."

Finally, elsewhere we read: "And He has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, all from Him" (وَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً مِنْهُ - Al-Jathiyah: 13).

From the sum of these verses, it is clearly understood that, firstly, man is the most evolved creature of this world, and from the perspective of the Islamic worldview, he has been given such value and status that all other creatures have been subjected to this human—the human who is the "Vicegerent of Allah" (Khalifatullah) and whose heart is the seat of Allah's light!

Secondly, it becomes clear that "subjugation" (taskhir) in these verses does not mean that man brings all these creatures under his direct command; rather, the fact that they move in the direction of his interests and service—for example, the celestial bodies radiate light for him or have other benefits—means they are subjected to him.

No school of thought has considered such high value for man, and in no philosophy does man have this much status and personality. This is one of the characteristics of the school of Islam, which raises the existential value of man to this extent. Awareness of this has a deep educational effect, for when a person thinks that God has given him all this greatness, and that the clouds, wind, moon, sun, and firmament are all at work, and all are wandering, subservient, and servants to him, such a person does not surrender to negligence and baseness, nor does he make himself a captive of desires or a slave to wealth, status, gold, or force. He breaks the chains and flies to the heights of the heavens.

How can it be said that the sun and the moon are not subjected to man, while through their radiance they make the scene of human life bright, warm, and ready? If the light of the sun did not exist, there would be no movement or motion on the planet Earth. On the other hand, through its gravity, it regulates the movement of the earth in its orbit and creates tides in the seas in cooperation with the moon, which is itself a source of many blessings and benefits.

Ships, seas, rivers, nights, and days—each in its own way serves man and moves in the path of his interests.

Reflection on these subjugations and their calculated system is a clear proof of the greatness, power, and wisdom of the Creator.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِن تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا أَءِنَّا لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٥)
If you are to wonder [at anything], then wonderful is their remark, ‘When we have become dust, shall we be [ushered] into a new creation?’ They are the ones who defy their Lord; they shall have iron collars around their necks, they shall be the inhabitants of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (5)
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِمُ ٱلْمَثُلَـٰتُ ۗ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِّلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ ظُلْمِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٦)
They would press you for evil sooner than for good, though there have already gone by before them exemplary punishments. Indeed your Lord is forgiving to mankind despite their wrongdoing, and indeed your Lord is severe in retribution. (6)

Commentary

The Disbelievers' Amazement at the Resurrection

Following the discussion regarding the signs of Allah's greatness in the preceding verses, the first verse under discussion addresses the issue of "Resurrection." It strengthens this discourse through the specific connection and continuity that exists between the origin (Creation) and the return (Resurrection), stating:

"And if you wonder, then wondrous is their saying: 'When we are dust, shall we indeed then be in a new creation?'" (وَ إِنْ تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ أَ إِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَ إِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ) [1] This is the same amazement that all ignorant nations had regarding the issue of Resurrection, perceiving new creation and life after death as impossible. This is while the previous verses and other verses of the Quran have answered this issue well: what is the difference between the beginning of creation and the renewal of creation? The same One who was capable of creating them in the beginning is capable of once again clothing their frames with the garment of existence and life. It is as if they have forgotten their own initial creation that they now debate and dispute its renewal.

Then, He describes the current state and future destiny of this group in three sentences:

First, He says: "Those are they who disbelieved in their Lord" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ).

For if they had accepted Allah and His Lordship, they would never have doubted His power regarding the issue of Resurrection and the renewal of human life. Therefore, the ruin of their affair regarding the Resurrection is born from the ruin of their affair regarding the Oneness (Tawhid) and Lordship of God.

Secondly, due to disbelief, lack of faith, and exiting from under the banner of the freedom of Tawhid, they have ensnared themselves in shackles and chains, placing the chains of idol-worship, whims, materialism, ignorance, and superstitions around their own necks with their own hands.

[1]: The phrase "إِنْ تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ" actually means: if you wish to wonder at anything, wonder at their speech, for it is truly a cause for amazement. In fact, "فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ" is the recompense (jaza) for the conditional sentence. Another possibility in the meaning of the above phrase is: "If you wonder at their idol-worship, it is not only their idol-worship that is a cause for wonder, but their denial of the Resurrection is also a cause for wonder." However, the first meaning seems more correct.

"And those—the shackles are around their necks" (وَ أُولئِكَ الْأَغْلالُ فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ).

"Such individuals, with this state and this position, are certainly the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally," and no other result or expectation exists for them (وَ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ).

In the next verse, He addresses another illogical statement of the polytheists and says: "They ask you to hasten the evil before the good" (وَ يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ). Instead of requesting mercy from Allah through you, they demand the hastening of torment, retribution, and punishment.

Why is this people so stubborn and ignorant? Why do they not say, "If you are truthful, bring down the mercy of Allah upon us in such and such a way," but instead say, "If your word is true, send down the punishment of Allah upon us!"?

Do they think the divine punishment is a lie? "While there have already passed before them exemplary punishments" (وَ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمُ الْمَثُلاتُ) [1], the news of which is recorded on the pages of history and within the heart of the earth. Then He adds: "And indeed, your Lord is full of forgiveness for the people despite their wrongdoing, and indeed, your Lord is severe in retribution" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِلنَّاسِ عَلى‌ ظُلْمِهِمْ وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ).

The severity of His punishment will never prevent His general mercy, just as His general mercy should not lead to the mistake that He gives oppressors the opportunity to do whatever they wish, for in such cases He is severe in retribution. Attaining the effects of either of these two attributes of the Lord—namely, being "Full of Forgiveness" and "Severe in Retribution"—

[1]: Mathulat is the plural of mathulah, meaning the calamities and punishments that befell previous nations such that they became "proverbial" (darb al-mathal).

depends on the grounds that exist within the human being themselves.

Key Points

1. Why Wonder at the New Creation?

It is understood from various verses of the Quran that one of the difficulties prophets faced against polytheistic nations was proving the issue of "Physical Resurrection." They always wondered how a human, after becoming dust, could return to life and existence. This same expression seen in the verses under discussion (أَ إِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَ إِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ) is seen with slight variations in seven other places in the Quran (Al-Mu'minun: 35, Al-Mu'minun: 82, Al-Naml: 67, As-Saffat: 16 and 53, Qaf: 3, Al-Waqi'ah: 47).

From this, it becomes clear that this objection was very significant in their view, as they emphasized it everywhere. However, the Holy Quran gives them a decisive answer in very short phrases. For example, in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 29, with the phrase كَما بَدَأَكُمْ تَعُودُونَ (As He began you, so shall you return), which is no more than a few words, it gives a crushing response to this subject. Elsewhere it says: وَ هُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ (And it is easier for Him) (Ar-Rum: 27), because in the beginning you were nothing, and now at least decayed bones or dust exist of you.

In some instances, He takes people by the hand and urges them to study the greatness and power of Allah in the creation of this vast earth and heavens: "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the likes of them?" (Ya-Sin: 81).

2. Does Allah Forgive the Oppressors?

In the above verses, we read that the Lord possesses forgiveness for people despite the wrongdoing they commit. Certainly, the meaning is not that Allah includes an oppressor who persists in his oppression in His pardon; rather, He wishes to give the oppressors the possibility to return and reform themselves through this means. Otherwise, they will be subject to the second phrase which says your Lord is "Severe in Retribution."

Furthermore, it is inferred from this verse that major sins (including oppression) are also forgivable (provided all conditions are met). This verse and its likes provide a decisive answer to the incorrect statement reported from the "Mu'tazilites" of old, who claimed that major sins would never be forgiven.

In any case, the mention of the Lord's "Vast Forgiveness" and His "Severe Retribution" is actually to place everyone on the middle path between "Fear" and "Hope," which is an important factor in human education. They should never despair of Allah's mercy, no matter how heavy their crime, nor should they ever consider themselves safe from His punishment, no matter how light their sin.

Hence, we read in a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"If it were not for Allah's pardon and His overlooking, life would never be pleasant for anyone; and if it were not for Allah's threat and His punishment, everyone would rely [solely] on His mercy and do whatever they wished." [1] From here it becomes clear that those who, while committing sins, arrogantly say:

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 5 & 6, p. 278, under the verse; Tafsir al-Qurtubi, vol. 6, p. 3514.

"God is Generous (Karim)," have not actually relied on God's generosity; they are lying and are actually indifferent to the Lord's retribution.

Ayah

وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هَادٍ (٧)
The faithless say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people. (7)

Commentary

Once Again, Pretext-Seeking!

After the previous verses alluded to the issue of "Monotheism" and made a reference to the "Resurrection," the verse under discussion addresses one of the objections of the stubborn polytheists regarding "Prophethood." It states: "And those who disbelieve say: 'Why has a sign (miracle) not been sent down to him from his Lord?'" (وَ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

It is clear that one of the duties of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is to present miracles as a document of his truthfulness and his connection to Divine revelation. Truth-seeking people have the right to demand a miracle when in doubt regarding the call to Prophethood, unless the proofs of Prophethood are evident through other means.

However, one must pay close attention to a subtle point: the opponents of the Prophets did not always possess good intentions. That is, they did not seek miracles to find the truth; rather, out of stubbornness and a refusal to submit to the truth, they would constantly propose strange and extraordinary miracles.

These types of miracles, known as "suggested miracles" (mu'jizat iqtirahi), were never intended for discovering the truth of Prophethood. For this reason, the Prophets never submitted to them. In reality, this group of stubborn disbelievers thought that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله claimed, "I am capable of doing everything and I am a performer of the supernatural! I am sitting here so that whoever proposes any extraordinary matter, I shall perform it for them!"

But the Prophets, by mentioning the fact that miracles are in the hand of Allah and are performed by His command, and that our duty is the education and guidance of the people, would reject such individuals.

Therefore, in the verse under discussion, we read that following this statement, He says: O Prophet, "You are only a warner, and for every people there is a guide" (إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَ لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ).

Here, two questions arise

1- How can the sentence "إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَ لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ" be an answer to the disbelievers regarding their request for a miracle?

The answer to this question is clear in light of what we mentioned above. For the Prophet is not a performer of the supernatural who performs miracles at the request of anyone and for any purpose or intent. His primary duty is "Inzar" (warning)—that is, giving warning to those who go astray and inviting them to the Straight Path. Of course, whenever there is a need for a miracle to complete this warning and to bring the lost to the Straight Path, the Prophet will certainly not fall short. However, he has no such duty toward the stubborn who are not on this path.

In fact, the Quran says: These disbelievers have forgotten the primary duty of the Prophet, which is the issue of warning and inviting toward Allah, and they have imagined that his primary duty is performing miracles.

2- What is meant by the phrase "لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ"?

A group of commentators has said that both these attributes, "Warner" (Munzir) and "Guide" (Hadi), refer back to the Prophet, and in reality, the sentence was: "You are a warner and a guide for every population."

However, this interpretation goes against the apparent meaning of the verse, because the "and" (waw) has separated the phrase "لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ" from "إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ". Yes, if the word "Hadi" had come before "لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ", this meaning would be completely acceptable, but it is not so.

Furthermore, the objective was to describe two types of callers toward the Truth: first, the caller whose work is warning (Inzar), and second, the caller whose work is guidance (Hidayah).

You will surely ask: what is the difference between "Inzar" and "Hidayah"?

In response, we say that "Inzar" is for the purpose of bringing the lost from the wrong path back to the right way and placing them within the Straight Path. But "Hidayah" (guidance) is for the purpose of leading the people forward after they have entered the path.

In reality, the "Warner" is like the "originating cause" (al-illat al-muhdithah) and the initiator, while the "Guide" is in the position of the "sustaining cause" (al-illat al-mubqiyah), the protector, and the one who advances them. This is exactly what we express as "Messenger" (Rasul) and "Imam." The Messenger establishes the Sharia, and the Imam is the protector and guardian of the Sharia. (There is no doubt that the term "Guide" has been applied to the person of the Prophet in other instances, but by the context of mentioning "Warner" in the above verse, we understand that the "Guide" refers to the one who continues the path of the Prophet and is the protector and guardian of his Sharia).

Numerous narrations reported from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in both Shia and Sunni books, stating: "I am the warner and Ali is the guide," fully support this interpretation. As an example, we mention a few of these narrations:

1- "Fakhr al-Razi," under this very verse in his commentary, narrates this sentence from "Ibn Abbas" as follows: The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله placed his hand on his chest and said: "I am the warner," then he gestured toward the shoulder of Ali عليه السلام and said: "You are the guide; through you, the guided ones shall find guidance after me." [^1] This narration has been reported by the famous Sunni scholar Allamah Ibn Kathir in his Tafsir, as well as Allamah Ibn Sabbagh al-Maliki in "Al-Fusul al-Muhimmah," Ganji al-Shafi'i in "Kifayat al-Talib," Tabari in his Tafsir, Abu Hayyan al-Andalusi in his book of Tafsir named "Al-Bahr al-Muhit," as well as Allamah Nishaburi in his Tafsir, and a group of others.

2- Hamuwayni, who is among the famous Sunni scholars, narrates from Abu Hurayrah al-Aslami in the book "Fara'id al-Simtayn": "Indeed, the intended meaning of the Guide is Ali عليه السلام."

3- Mir Ghiyath al-Din, the author of the book "Habib al-Siyar," writes in the second volume of his book, page 12: "It has been established through multiple chains that when the words of the Almighty 'إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَ لِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ' were revealed, he said to Ali: 'I am the warner and you are the guide; through you, O Ali, the guided ones shall find guidance after me.'"

Alusi in "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Shiblanji in "Nur al-Absar," and Sheikh Sulayman Qunduzi in "Yanabi' al-Mawaddah" have also narrated this hadith with the same wording or something close to it.

Although the narrator of this hadith in most of its chains is Ibn Abbas, it is not limited to Ibn Abbas; rather, it has also been narrated from Abu Hurayrah (according to Hamuwayni's report) and from Ali عليه السلام himself (according to Tha'labi's report), where he said: "The warner is the Prophet, and the guide is a man from Banu Hashim," meaning himself. [^1] Although these hadiths do not explicitly state the issue of Wilayah and immediate succession, considering that guidance in the broad sense of the word was not limited to Ali عليه السلام—rather, all true scholars and special companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله performed this program—it becomes clear that the introduction of Ali عليه السلام as "The Guide" is due to a distinction and specialty he possessed. He was the supreme manifestation of the Guide, and such a matter would not be separate from the Wilayah and succession of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 19, p. 14. [^1]: For further explanation, refer to the precious book Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 3, p. 87 onwards, and various books of Tafsir by Sunni and Shia scholars.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنثَىٰ وَمَا تَغِيضُ ٱلْأَرْحَامُ وَمَا تَزْدَادُ ۖ وَكُلُّ شَىْءٍ عِندَهُۥ بِمِقْدَارٍ (٨)
Allah knows what every female carries [in her womb], and what the wombs reduce and what they increase, and everything is by [precise] measure with Him, (8)
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْكَبِيرُ ٱلْمُتَعَالِ (٩)
the Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, the All-great, the All-sublime. (9)
سَوَآءٌ مِّنكُم مَّنْ أَسَرَّ ٱلْقَوْلَ وَمَن جَهَرَ بِهِۦ وَمَنْ هُوَ مُسْتَخْفٍۭ بِٱلَّيْلِ وَسَارِبٌۢ بِٱلنَّهَارِ (١٠)
It is the same [to Him] whether any of you speaks secretly, or does so loudly, or whether he lurks in the night, or is open to view in daytime. (10)

Commentary

Allah’s Infinite Knowledge

In this section of the verses, we read about a portion of the Lord’s attributes that completes both the discussion on Monotheism (Tawhid) and the discussions on the Hereafter (Ma'ad).

The discourse concerns the vast knowledge of the Lord and His awareness of all things—the very knowledge that is the source of the order of creation, the wonders of existence, and the proofs of Monotheism; the same knowledge that is the foundation of the Resurrection and the Great Court of Judgment. In these verses, emphasis is placed on both aspects: knowledge of the system of creation and knowledge of the deeds of His servants.

First, it states: "Allah knows what every female—whether human or animal—carries in her womb" (اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى‌).

"And He also knows that which the wombs fall short of and discharge before the appointed time" (وَ ما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ‌) [^1] and "He is also aware of that which they exceed beyond the appointed term" (وَ ما تَزْدادُ).

There is much discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of the three phrases above:

Some, as we mentioned above, consider them an allusion to the three states of pregnancy: sometimes the birth occurs at the appointed time, sometimes before the time (as if the womb has "swallowed" or diminished the required duration), and sometimes it occurs after the appointed term. Allah knows all of these and is aware of the date and moment of the fetus's birth without the slightest deficiency or excess. This is among the matters that no person or apparatus can precisely determine; this knowledge is exclusive to the Pure Essence of the Lord. The reason for this is clear, as the capacities of wombs and fetuses are entirely different, and no one is precisely aware of these differences.

Others have said that the three phrases above refer to three different states of the "womb's" functions during the days of pregnancy: the first phrase refers to the fetus itself which the womb preserves; the second phrase refers to the menstrual blood that flows into it and is absorbed by the fetus, which sucks it in and "swallows" it; and the third phrase refers to the extra blood that is occasionally discharged during the days of pregnancy or expelled at the time of birth and thereafter. [^1] Other possibilities have also been suggested in the interpretation of the verse, none of which are contradictory to one another; it is possible that the above verse refers to the sum of these interpretations, although the first interpretation appears more prominent, as the phrase "carries" (tahmil) implies the carrying of the fetus, and the phrases "fall short" (taghid) and "exceed" (tazdad)—by context—refer to the decrease or increase in the duration of pregnancy.

In a hadith narrated in al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام or Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read:

"The ghayd (falling short) is every pregnancy born in less than nine months, and ma tazdad (what exceeds) is everything that exceeds nine months."

The hadith continues:

"Whenever a woman sees pure blood during her pregnancy, her pregnancy term increases by the number of days she saw that blood!" [^2]

Then the Quran adds: "Everything with Him is by a fixed and determined measure" (وَ كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ).

This is so it is not imagined that these increases and decreases are without account or reason; rather, every hour, second, and moment is calculated, just as the parts of the fetus and the blood of the womb are all subject to precise accounting.

The next verse is, in fact, a proof for what was stated in the previous verse; it says: "Allah knows the unseen and the seen (the hidden and the manifest)" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ).

His awareness of the unseen and the seen is because "He is the Great, the Most High, and Dominant over everything," and for this reason, He is present everywhere, and nothing is hidden from the eyes of His knowledge (الْكَبِيرُ الْمُتَعالِ‌).

To complete this discussion and emphasize His infinite knowledge, it adds: "It makes no difference to Allah whether one of you conceals his speech or declares it openly"; He knows and hears all (سَواءٌ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ وَ مَنْ جَهَرَ بِهِ‌). "And likewise, it makes no difference to Him whether one hides by night within the veils of darkness or goes forth openly in the broad daylight following his business" (وَ مَنْ هُوَ مُسْتَخْفٍ بِاللَّيْلِ وَ سارِبٌ بِالنَّهارِ). [^1] Fundamentally, for One who is present everywhere, light and darkness, obscurity and brightness, unseen and seen have no meaning; He is equally aware and informed of all these.

Key Points

1. The Quran and Embryology

In the Holy Quran, the issue of the fetus and its wonders, marvels, and systems is repeatedly mentioned as a proof of Monotheism, the recognition of God, and the infinite knowledge of the Truth. Of course, embryology as a science is very new and young; in the past, scientists had limited information about the fetus and its various stages. However, with the advancement of science and knowledge, an extraordinary leap occurred in this field, and numerous secrets and wonders of this silent and quiet world were revealed to us, such that it can be said: a world of lessons in Monotheism and the recognition of God lies hidden in the creation of the fetus and its stages of transformation and perfection.

Who else could attend to a being that is out of everyone's reach—and in the Quran's expression, is placed within "three darknesses"—whose life is exceptionally delicate and precise, providing it with the necessary nutrients and guiding it through all stages?

In the above verses, when it says: "Allah knows what every female carries in her womb," the meaning is not merely that He is aware of its gender (i.e., male or female), but rather He is aware of all its characteristics, talents, tastes, and the powers potentially hidden within it—matters that no one can become aware of by any means.

Therefore, the existence of these calculated systems in the fetus and its leadership along the path of its precise and complex perfection is impossible except from an All-Knowing and All-Powerful Origin.

2. Everything Has a Measure

In various verses of the Holy Quran, we read that everything is limited to a boundary that it does not exceed. In Surah At-Talaq, verse 3, He says: "Allah has indeed set a measure for everything" (قَدْ جَعَلَ اللَّهُ لِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدْراً). And in Surah Al-Hijr, verse 21, we read: "And there is not a thing but with Us are its treasuries, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَ ما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ‌). In the verses under discussion, we also read: "And everything with Him is by a measure" (وَ كُلُّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ).

These all point to the fact that nothing in this universe is without account. Even the entities that we sometimes assume in the world of nature to be without order or calculation all have a precise account, whether we know it or not. Fundamentally, Allah being "All-Wise" (Hakim) has no meaning other than that everything in His creation has a program, a limit, and a measure.

What we have discovered today of the secrets of creation through the sciences fully confirms this reality. For example, human blood—which is his most vital substance and responsible for delivering all necessary materials to all cells of the human body—is composed of over twenty substances. The ratio of these materials and the measure and quality of each is so precise that with the slightest change, human health is endangered. For this reason, to identify bodily deficiencies, one immediately turns to blood tests and the measurement of sugar, fats, urea, iron, and other components, and from the deficiency or excess of these parts, the causes of bodily failures and diseases are immediately discovered.

It is not only human blood that has such a precise composition; this precision and calculation exist throughout the entire universe.

Furthermore, by noting this point, it becomes clear that what we sometimes perceive as disorders or irregularities in the universe are actually related to the inadequacy of our science and knowledge. A true monotheist and God-worshiper can never have such a conception of the universe, and the gradual progress of sciences bears witness to this reality.

We can also learn this lesson: that human society—which is a part of the total system of existence—if it wishes to live healthily, must have this principle ("Everything with Him is by a measure") governing its entirety. It must avoid all forms of excess, negligence, and actions that lack calculation, and establish accountability and calculation in all social institutions.

3. The Unseen and the Seen are Equal to Allah

In the verses under discussion, emphasis is placed on the fact that the unseen and the seen are clear before Allah. Fundamentally, "unseen" (ghayb) and "seen" (shuhud) are two relative concepts used regarding a being whose knowledge and existence are limited. For instance, we possess senses; whatever falls within the range of our sight, hearing, and other senses is "seen" for us, and whatever is outside the sphere of our seeing and hearing is considered "unseen" relative to us. If, hypothetically, our power of sight were unlimited and penetrated the interior and essence of objects and the particles of the world, everything would be "seen" for us.

Since everything other than the Pure Essence of Allah is limited, the unseen and the seen exist for all of them. But because Allah's Essence is unlimited and present everywhere, everything is "seen" for Him, and "unseen" has no meaning regarding His Pure Essence. If we say Allah is the "Knower of the Unseen and the Seen," it means that what is considered unseen or seen for us is equal and "seen" for Him.

Suppose we look at the palm of our hand in the light; is it possible for us to be unaware of what is on it? The universe is far more clear and manifest than this before the knowledge of Allah.

4. The Educational Effects of Mindfulness of Allah’s Knowledge

When we read in the above verses that Allah knows secret and open speech, and your daily and nightly comings and goings and movements equally, and all are manifest before His knowledge—if we truly believe in this reality and feel His constant supervision over us, a profound transformation will occur in our spirit, thought, speech, and deeds.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, in response to someone asking "What is your life's program?", he mentioned several things, including:

"I learned that Allah is aware of me, so I felt bashful [to sin]."

In the history of Islam and the daily lives of committed Muslims, we observe many manifestations of this reality.

It is said that a father and son entered a garden. The father, intending to pick fruit without the owner's permission, climbed a tree. His son, who was a youth of spiritual insight, called out, "Father, come down!" The father was startled, composed himself, and immediately came down. He asked, "I didn't see who it was that saw me." The son said, "Someone from above your head!" The father looked up and saw nothing. The son said, "I mean Allah, who is above and encompasses us all. How is it possible to be terrified of a human's gaze, yet not be terrified that Allah sees you in every state? What kind of faith is this?!"

Ayah

لَهُۥ مُعَقِّبَـٰتٌ مِّنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ يَحْفَظُونَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُغَيِّرُ مَا بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۗ وَإِذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوٓءًا فَلَا مَرَدَّ لَهُۥ ۚ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَالٍ (١١)
He has guardian angels, to his front and his rear, who guard him by Allah’s command. Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls. And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it, and they have no protector besides Him. (11)

Commentary

Divine Guardians!

In the preceding verses, we read that Allah, by virtue of being the Knower of the Unseen and the Witness, is aware of people's hidden and manifest affairs and is present and observant everywhere.

In the verse under discussion, it adds that in addition to this, Allah is also the Protector and Guardian of His servants: "For him [man] are successive [angels] before him and behind him who guard him by the command of Allah" (لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَ مِنْ خَلْفِهِ يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللَّهِ) [^1]

[^1]: There is discussion among commentators regarding whom the pronoun "for him" (lahu) refers to. The well-known and famous view is what we mentioned above—that it refers to "man," who was alluded to in the previous verses. Some have suggested it might refer to the Prophet or to Allah, but these two possibilities are inconsistent with the end of the verse (reflect carefully).

However, lest anyone mistakenly think that this protection and guardianship is unconditional—and that man can throw himself into precipices, engage in any folly, or commit any sin deserving of punishment and torment while still expecting Allah and His agents to protect him—it adds: "Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves!" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ).

Furthermore, to prevent the misunderstanding of how divine punishment and calamities make sense despite the existence of divine agents responsible for protecting man, the end of the verse adds: "And when Allah intends for a people ill, there is no repelling it" (وَ إِذا أَرادَ اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً فَلا مَدَرَّ لَهُ).

"And there is not for them besides Him any patron [or helper]" (وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ والٍ).

For this reason, when Allah's command for the punishment, chastisement, or destruction of a tribe or nation is issued, the guardians and protectors move away and surrender the person to the events!

Key Points

1- What are the "Mu'aqqibat"?

"Mu'aqqibat," as Tabarsi stated in Majma' al-Bayan and as other great commentators have noted, is the plural of "mu'aqqibah," which in turn is the plural of "mu'aqqib," meaning a group that follows one another successively and alternately to perform a task.

The apparent meaning of this verse is that Allah has commissioned a group of angels to come to man alternately by night and day, acting as his guardians and protectors from before him and behind him.

Undoubtedly, man in his life is exposed to many pests and calamities; events from within and without, various diseases, microbes, and types of accidents and dangers springing from the earth and sky surround him. Especially during childhood, when man's awareness of his surroundings is very meager and he has no experience, danger lies in ambush at every step. Sometimes one wonders how a child survives such a multitude of incidents and grows up, especially in families where parents lack much knowledge of these matters or lack resources, such as children growing up in villages amidst a mass of deprivations, disease factors, and dangers.

If we truly reflect on these matters, we feel there is a protective force guarding us against accidents, acting like a shield from before and behind us.

On many occasions, dangerous incidents occur for a person, and he escapes them miraculously, such that he feels all of this is not a coincidence but rather a protective force guarding him.

This has also been emphasized in numerous narrations quoted from the leaders of Islam, including:

In a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the above verse:

"He [the angel] protects him by Allah's command from falling into a well, or a wall falling upon him, or anything hitting him, until the fixed decree (Qadar) arrives; then they leave him to it, surrendering him to the decrees. They are two angels who protect him by night and two angels by day who perform this duty alternately." [^1] In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"There is no servant except that there are two angels with him protecting him. But when the command comes from Allah, they leave him to the command of Allah." [^2] (Therefore, they only protect him from events that have not become finalized by Allah's command).

In Nahj al-Balagha, we also read that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

"With every human being there are two angels protecting him, but when the fixed decree arrives, they leave him to it." [^3] Likewise, in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, describing the angels and their various groups, we read:

"And among them are the guardians of His servants." Of course, the lack of awareness of these angels through the senses or through natural sciences can never be a reason to negate them, for this is not limited to our current discussion. The Holy Quran, as well as other religions, has informed us of many matters beyond human sense that mankind cannot know through ordinary means.

Beyond this, as mentioned above, we see clear signs of such a protective force in our daily lives and feel that we are

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 283.

[^3]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 201.

miraculously saved from many deadly incidents, the explanation and justification of which are difficult through ordinary means or by attributing them to coincidence. (I have personally seen some examples of this in life that were truly astonishing, serving as proof for the existence of that invisible protector even for a skeptical person like me!)

2- Changes always stem from ourselves! (A General Law).

The phrase "Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves," which appears in two places in the Quran with slight differences, expresses a general and universal law—a destiny-shaping, movement-creating, and warning law! This law, which is one of the fundamental pillars of worldview and sociology in Islam, tells us that your destiny is, before anything and anyone else, in your own hands. Any kind of change and transformation in the happiness or misery of nations primarily returns to themselves. Luck, horoscopes, fortune, coincidence, and the influence of celestial bodies and the like have no basis. What is fundamental and foundational is that a nation itself must want to be honorable, proud, victorious, and progressive, or conversely, it must submit itself to humiliation, weakness, and defeat. Even Allah's grace, or His punishment, will not affect any nation without cause; rather, it is the will and desire of nations and their internal changes that make them deserving of Allah's grace or worthy of His punishment.

In other words: This Quranic principle, which expresses one of the most important social programs of Islam, tells us that any external changes are dependent on the internal changes of nations and peoples. Any victory or defeat that reaches a people originates from here. Therefore, those who always look for "external factors" to exonerate themselves, and always consider dominant and colonizing powers as the cause of their misery, are gravely mistaken. For if these hellish powers do not have a base

within a society, they can achieve nothing.

The important thing is to crush the bases of the dominators, colonizers, and tyrants within our own society so that they have no way to penetrate.

They are like Satan, and we know that according to the Quran, Satan has no way over those who are the sincere servants of Allah; he only prevails over those who have built a base for Satan within their own being.

This Quranic principle says: To end miseries and failures, we must start a revolution from within—an intellectual and cultural revolution, a revolution of faith and morality. When caught in the clutches of misery, we must immediately search for our own points of weakness and wash them from the garment of our soul and spirit with the water of repentance and a return to the Truth. We must experience a new birth and a new light and movement, so that in its light we can turn failures and defeats into victory, rather than letting these points of weakness—which are the factors of defeat—remain hidden under the covers of selfishness while we wander aimlessly searching for the factors of defeat outside our society! To date, many books or articles have been written about the factors behind the victory of the early Muslims and the factors behind the retreat of Muslims in later centuries, many of whose discussions resemble searching in rocky terrain and dead ends. If we wish to take inspiration from the above principle which has reached us from the source of revelation, we must seek both that victory and that defeat and failure in the intellectual, ideological, and moral changes and the practical programs of Muslims, and nothing else. In contemporary revolutions, including the revolution of our nation (the Muslims of Iran), the Algerian revolution, the Afghan revolution, and the like, we clearly observe the sovereignty of this Quranic principle.

That is, without the colonial governments and dominant superpowers changing their methods, when we were transformed from within, everything changed.

In any case, this is a lesson for today, tomorrow, and all future tomorrows, for all Muslims and all future generations! We see that only those leaders were victorious and successful who led and transformed their nation based on this principle. Islamic history and contemporary history are full of evidence for this fundamental and eternal principle, the mention of which would take us away from our method of discussion in this Tafsir.

Commentary

Another Aspect of the Signs of His Greatness

Here, the Quran once again addresses the verses of Monotheism (## Commentary

Another Aspect of the Signs of His Greatness

Here, the Quran once again addresses the verses of Monotheism, the signs of Allah's greatness, and the secrets of creation. By pointing to various parts of natural phenomena and providing short yet meaningful references to their secrets and properties, it brings the relationship of the servants closer to Allah and sprinkles the light of faith and knowledge into their hearts.

First, it refers to lightning (the flash that appears between pieces of clouds) and says: "It is He who shows you the lightning, as a cause of 'fear' and 'hope'!" (هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَ طَمَعاً).

On one hand, its brilliant radiance dazzles the eyes, and the terrifying sound of thunder arising from it sometimes strikes you with horror. The fear and anxiety of fire hazards resulting from it—especially for those living in or traveling through deserts—distress them.

However, since it is often accompanied by downpours that grant pure water to the thirsty inhabitants of the desert and saturate trees and crops, it leads them toward hope and expectation. In the midst of this fear and hope, they pass through sensitive moments.

Then it adds: "It is He who brings up the heavy and laden clouds" which are capable of irrigating thirsty lands (وَ يُنْشِئُ السَّحابَ الثِّقالَ).

The Blessings of Thunder and Lightning

We know scientifically that the occurrence of lightning is due to two pieces of clouds with different electrical charges (positive and negative) approaching each other. Just like the ends of two electric wires that spark when brought close, they also create a massive spark and undergo what is called an electrical discharge.

If the small sparks visible to our eyes from the ends of two wires produce a faint sound, the sound of the celestial spark of lightning—due to the expansion of the clouds and the high level of electricity—is so intense that it creates "thunder."

Whenever a piece of cloud carrying a positive charge approaches the earth, which always carries a negative charge, a spark is created between the earth and the cloud, which is called a "bolt" or "thunderbolt" (sa'iqah). Its danger lies in the fact that one end is the earth and high points that form the "tip" of this wire. Even a human in a desert may practically become the tip of this negative wire, and a terrifying spark may descend upon them, turning them into ashes in a brief moment. For this reason, during thunder and lightning in deserts, one must immediately take refuge near a tree, a wall, a mountain, or any high point, or lie down in a hollow.

In any case, lightning, which some might consider a mere "jest of nature," has been proven by modern scientific discoveries to have numerous benefits and blessings, three of which are mentioned below:

  1. Irrigation: Lightning usually produces extraordinary heat, sometimes around 15,000 degrees Celsius! This heat is sufficient to burn a large amount of the surrounding air, causing the air pressure to drop immediately. We know that clouds rain in low pressure; therefore, downpours often begin following a flash of lightning, and large raindrops fall. Thus, one of the functions of lightning is actually irrigation.

  2. Disinfection: When lightning appears with its intense heat, raindrops combine with extra oxygen and create heavy water, i.e., hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$). We know that one of the effects of hydrogen peroxide is killing microbes; hence, it is used in medical applications for washing wounds. When these drops of hydrogen peroxide rain upon the earth, they destroy the eggs of pests and plant diseases, providing an excellent disinfection. For this reason, it is said that in years with less thunder and lightning, plant pests are more prevalent.

  3. Nutrition and Fertilization: Raindrops, due to lightning and intense heat and chemical combination, acquire a carbonic acid state. When they are sprinkled on the ground and combine with it, they form a type of effective plant fertilizer.

Plants are nourished through this process. Some scientists have stated that the amount of fertilizer produced annually by the total lightning across the globe reaches tens of millions of tons! This is an extraordinarily high figure.

Therefore, we see how this seemingly trivial and useless phenomenon of nature is so productive and blessed: it irrigates, it disinfects, and it nourishes. This is a small example of the wondrous and vast secrets of the universe that can serve as a clear guide to the issue of knowing Allah.

All these are the blessings of lightning on one hand; on the other hand, the fires caused by one type of it (the thunderbolt) may sometimes burn a person, people, or trees. Although this is rare and avoidable, it can be a cause of fear and dread. Thus, what we read in the above verse—that lightning is a cause of both fear and hope—may refer to the sum of these matters.

It is also possible that the phrase "and He brings up the heavy clouds" mentioned at the end of the above verse is related to this property of lightning, which makes the clouds heavy with dense raindrops.

The next verse addresses the sound of "thunder," which is never separated from lightning, and says:

"Thunder glorifies and praises Allah" (وَ يُسَبِّحُ الرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِ). Yes, this resonant sound of the natural world, which is a proverb for the greatness of sound, practically glorifies Allah because it is coupled with the phenomenon of lightning and both serve a single purpose with calculated and valuable services mentioned above. In other words, "thunder" is the eloquent tongue of "lightning," narrating the system of creation and the greatness of the Creator.

This is what we call the "language of state" (zaban-e-hal): a content-rich book, a brilliant poem, a beautiful and soul-stirring painting, a sturdy and well-ordered building—all speak through their state of the knowledge, skill, and taste of the writer, poet, painter, and architect; they praise and extol them.

The particles of this universe, all of which are mysterious and have a very precise and calculated system, all speak of Allah's purity and His being free from any defect or flaw (is "glorification" [tasbih] anything other than declaring purity?). And they all report His power, knowledge, and wisdom (is "praise" [hamd] anything other than expressing attributes of perfection?). [^1] A group of philosophers has also suggested the possibility that all particles of this world each possess a type of intellect and consciousness, and based on this intellect and consciousness, they glorify and sanctify Allah—not only through the "language of state" and their existence narrating the existence of Allah, but also with the "language of speech" (zaban-e-qal).

Not only do the sound of thunder or other components of the material world glorify Him, but "all the angels also engage in His glorification out of fear and awe of Allah" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ مِنْ خِيفَتِهِ). [^1] They fear that they might fall short in carrying out the commands of the Lord and the responsibilities entrusted to them in the system of existence, thereby facing divine punishment. We know that duties and obligations always create fear for those who feel responsible—a constructive fear that drives a person toward effort and movement.

To further explain thunder and lightning, it refers to thunderbolts and says:

"Allah sends the thunderbolts and strikes with them whom He wills" (وَ يُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ فَيُصِيبُ بِها مَنْ يَشاءُ).

Yet, despite all this, and despite observing the signs of the Lord's greatness in the world of creation across the earth and sky, in plants and trees, and in thunder and lightning and the like, and despite the smallness and insignificance of human power against events—even against a single celestial spark—a group of the oblivious still rise to dispute and contend regarding Allah (وَ هُمْ يُجادِلُونَ فِي اللَّهِ).

While Allah possesses infinite power, painful punishment, and severe retribution (وَ هُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ).

[^1]: Further explanation regarding the "glorification and sanctification" of beings will come, Allah willing, under the noble verse: "And there is not a thing but glorifies Him with His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (Al-Isra, 44).

"Mihal" is originally from "hilah," and "hilah" means any kind of hidden or non-obvious planning or remedy (not in the sense of destructive schemes as it has become known in the Persian language). It is certain that one who has the ability for such planning, with power and intensity, is someone who is extraordinary both in terms of ability and in terms of knowledge and wisdom. For this reason, He is dominant and victorious over His enemies, and no one has the power to escape the sphere of His authority. Consequently, commentators have each interpreted "شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ" in a way that all actually stem from the above meaning: some have interpreted it as "Severe in Power," some as "Severe in Punishment," some as "Severe in Might," some as "Severe in Seizing," and the like. [^1]

The last verse under discussion points to two matters: First, that "the call of truth belongs to Allah" (لَهُ دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِ).

This means whenever we call upon Him, He hears and responds; He has both awareness of the prayers of His servants and the power to fulfill their requests. For this reason, calling Him and petitioning His Holy Essence is "truth" (haqq), not false or baseless.

The second is that calling upon idols and petitioning them is a false call and prayer, because "those whom the polytheists call besides Allah and to whom they take refuge for the fulfillment of their needs never answer them; they do not respond to their prayer" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُمْ بِشَيْ‌ءٍ).

Yes, such is the false call, for it is nothing more than a fancy and imagination, and any knowledge or power they attribute to idols is completely illusory, groundless, and baseless.

Is "truth" anything other than objectivity, reality, and a source of good and blessing, and "falsehood" anything other than fancy, illusion, and a source of evil and corruption? Then—as is the method of the Quran—to visualize this intellectual subject, it presents a beautiful and eloquent sensory example and says:

"Those who call upon other than Allah are like one who sits by water whose surface is out of his reach and gestures to it, hoping that the water will reach his mouth, but it never reaches it." What a futile dream and fancy! (إِلَّا كَباسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ إِلَى الْماءِ لِيَبْلُغَ فاهُ وَ ما هُوَ بِبالِغِهِ).

Can one sit by a well, stretch out a hand toward the water, and by gesturing, send the water to the mouth? Does anyone but a naive or insane person do such a thing?

There is also another possibility in interpreting the above phrase: it likens the idolaters to someone who holds his palms completely flat and horizontal and enters them into water, expecting the water to stay in his hand, whereas as soon as he takes his hand out of the water, the drops of water pour out from between his fingers and palm and nothing remains.

A third interpretation has also been given for this phrase: that the idolaters who go to idols to solve their problems are like someone who wants to hold water in his fist. Can water ever be held in a fist? This is taken from a famous proverb among the Arabs; when they want to give an example for someone who makes a futile effort, they say: huwa ka-qabid al-ma' bi-l-yad ("He is like one who wants to grab water with the hand").

The Arabic poet also says: Fa-asbahtu fima kana bayni wa baynaha / min al-wuddi mithla qabid al-ma' bi-l-yad ("My situation reached a point where, to preserve the affection between me and her, I was like one who wanted to hold water in his hand.") [^1]

However, the first interpretation seems the clearest of all! At the end of the verse, to emphasize this statement, it says: "The prayer of the disbelievers to the idols is nothing but a step taken in misguidance" (وَ ما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ).

What misguidance is greater than a person spending his effort and striving on a path that never leads him to the destination, becoming tired and exhausted but gaining no result or benefit?

[^1]: Some have not considered "mihal" to be from the root "hilah," but rather from the root "mahl" and "mahal," which means plotting, disputing, and deciding on punishment and retribution; however, what we stated in the text seems more correct, although both meanings are nearly similar in scope.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 5, p. 3529.

In the last verse under discussion, to show how the idolaters have separated from the caravan of the universe and become lost and alone in the wilderness, it says:

"To Allah prostrate all who are in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, as do their shadows, every morning and evening" (وَ لِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ طَوْعاً وَ كَرْهاً وَ ظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَ الْآصالِ).

Key Points

1- What is meant by the prostration of beings?

Prostration in such cases means humility and the ultimate modesty and submission. That is, all angels, humans, and all possessors of intellect and thought are humble before Allah and submissive to His command. However, for one group, their prostration and humility are only "existential" (takwini), meaning they are submissive to the laws of the universe and creation. But another group, in addition to existential prostration, also has "legislative" (tashri'i) prostration, meaning they prostrate before Allah by their own will and desire. For example, when we see that their bodies and souls are submissive to the laws of life, death, growth, health, illness, and the like, this state of submission and humility before the laws of creation is, in fact, a type of existential prostration on their part.

2- The expression "willingly or unwillingly" (طَوْعاً وَ كَرْهاً) may indicate that believers fall into prostration and show humility before the Lord out of desire and inclination, while non-believers—even if they are not willing to perform such a prostration—every particle of their being is submissive to Allah's command in terms of the laws of creation, whether they like it or not.

Additionally, it should be noted that "kurh" (with the vowel 'u') refers to a dislike that originates from within a person, while "karh" (with the vowel 'a') refers to a dislike caused by an external factor. In the subject under discussion, since non-believers are compelled by external factors inherent to the laws of creation, "karh" is used.
