Another possibility in interpreting "willingly or unwillingly" is that "willingly" refers to processes in the world of creation that agree with the innate and natural desire of a being (like the natural desire of a living being to stay alive), and "unwillingly" refers to a tendency imposed on a being from the outside, such as the death of a living being due to an invasion of microbes or other factors.

3- "Zilal" is the plural of "zill," meaning shadow. The mention of this word in the above verse shows that prostration is not limited to legislative prostration, because the shadows of beings have no will or choice of their own; rather, they are submissive to the laws of light radiation. Therefore, their prostration is existential, meaning submission to the laws of creation.

Of course, the mention of the word "zilal" (shadows) does not mean that all those in the heavens and the earth are material beings and possess shadows; rather, it only refers to that group of beings that have shadows. It is like saying, "The scholars of the city and their children attended such a gathering," meaning the children of those who had children; from this sentence, it is never inferred that all the scholars of the city possess children (pay close attention).

In any case, although a shadow is nothing more than a non-existent matter—which is the absence of light—since the presence of light surrounds it from every side, it has its own existence and effects. The explicit mention of this word in the above verse is perhaps for emphasis, that even the shadows of beings are humble before Allah.

4- "Asal" is the plural of "usul," and that is also the plural of "asil," which is derived from the root "asl" and means the end of the day, because it is considered the "asl" (root/basis) of the night! And "ghudu" is the plural of "ghadah," meaning the beginning of the day (and sometimes it is used as an infinitive).

Although the prostration and humility of the beings of the universe before Allah's command is not limited to morning and evening but is constant and at all hours, the mention of these two times is either as a metaphor for the permanence of this matter—just as we say "so-and-so is busy studying science every morning and evening," meaning he studies science always—or it is because the previous sentence mentioned the shadows of beings, and shadows show themselves more than any other time at the beginning and end of the day.

Ayah

قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا ۚ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَٱلنُّورُ ۗ أَمْ جَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ خَلَقُوا۟ كَخَلْقِهِۦ فَتَشَـٰبَهَ ٱلْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ قُلِ ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّـٰرُ (١٦)
Say, ‘Who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah!’ Say, ‘Have you then taken others besides Him for guardians, who have no control over their own benefit or harm?’ Say, ‘Are the blind one and the seer equal? Are darkness and light equal?’ Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed confusable to them? Say, ‘Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One and the All-paramount.’ (16)

Commentary

Why Idolatry?

Since the preceding verses contained extensive discussions regarding the recognition of God's existence, this verse addresses the error of the polytheists and idol-worshippers, pursuing this argument through several avenues.

First, addressing the Prophet, it says: "Ask them: Who is the Lord and Sustainer of the heavens and the earth?" (قُلْ مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Then, without the Prophet waiting for their response, He commands him to provide the answer himself: "Say: Allah" (قُلِ اللَّهُ‌).

Subsequently, He rebukes and reproaches them with this statement: "Say: Have you then taken besides Him protectors (and deities) as your reliance, even though these idols do not possess for themselves any benefit or harm?" (قُلْ أَ فَاتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ نَفْعاً وَ لا ضَرًّا).

In truth, He first argues through the "Lordship (Rububiyyah) of Allah"—that He is the Owner and Disposer of the universe, every good and blessing comes from Him, and He has the power to repel every evil and harm. This means that since you acknowledge He is the Creator and Lord, you must seek whatever you desire from Him, not from idols that are incapable of solving any of your problems.

Furthermore, He takes the matter a step higher, stating that they do not even possess benefit or harm for themselves, let alone for you. Given this, what knot can they untie for you that you turn to worship them? They are helpless in their own affairs; what then do you expect from them?

Then, by citing two clear and explicit examples, He distinguishes the state of the "Monotheist" (Muwahhid) and the "Polytheist" (Mushrik):

First, He says: "Say: Is the blind equal to the seeing?" (قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى‌ وَ الْبَصِيرُ).

Just as the blind and the seeing are not equal, so too the disbeliever and the believer are not equal, and idols cannot be placed alongside "Allah."

Secondly: "Or are the depths of darkness and the light equal?" (أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِي الظُّلُماتُ وَ النُّورُ).

How can darkness—which is the center of deviation, misguidance, error, and danger—be considered equal to light, which is guiding and life-giving? And how can idols, which are pure darkness, be placed alongside "God," who is the Absolute Light of the realm of existence? What affinity does faith and monotheism, which are the light of the soul and spirit, have with polytheism and idolatry, which are the source of darkness and gloom for the psyche?

Then, through another path, He makes the futility of the polytheists' belief even more evident, saying:

"Is it because those they associated with Allah created a creation like His creation, such that the [two] creations seemed alike to them, and they imagined that the idols deserve worship just as God does?" Because they do what God does! (أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

While this is not the case, and even the idol-worshippers do not hold such a belief about the idols; they too consider Allah the Creator of all things and regard the realm of creation as belonging entirely to Him.

Therefore, He immediately says: "Say: Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-Prevailing" (قُلِ اللَّهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ هُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

Key Points

1. Creatorship and Lordship are linked to Worthiness of Worship.

From the above verse, it is first understood that He who is the Creator is also the Lord and Sustainer, because creation is a continuous matter. It is not that Allah created beings and then stepped aside; rather, the grace of existence is perpetually issued from God, and every being receives existence from His Pure Essence moment by moment. Therefore, the plan of creation and the management of the universe, just like the beginning of creation, are all in the hand of God. For this reason, He is the Master of benefit and harm, and whatever others have is from Him. With this description, is anyone other than Allah worthy of servitude?

2. Why does He state both the question and the answer Himself?

Regarding the above verse, the question arises: why does Allah command His Prophet to ask the polytheists who the Lord of the heavens and the earth is, and then, without waiting for their response, command His Prophet to give the answer? And then immediately after that, He lashes out at the polytheists, asking why they worship idols? What kind of question-and-answer method is this?

However, by considering one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: sometimes the answer to a question is so obvious that there is no need to wait for a response from the other party. For example, we ask the person opposite us, "Is it night or day?" and immediately answer ourselves, "It is certainly night." This is a subtle metaphor indicating that the matter is so clear that it does not require waiting for an answer.

Furthermore, the polytheists reserved "Creatorship" (Khaliqiyyah) for Allah; they never said the idols were the creators of the heavens and the earth. Rather, they believed they were intercessors and capable of bringing benefit or harm to man, and for this reason, they believed they must be worshipped.

But since "Creatorship" is inseparable from "Lordship" (the management and administration of the realm of existence), the polytheists can be held to this logic: you who consider Creatorship exclusive to God must also consider Lordship exclusive to Him, and consequently, worship is also exclusive to Him.

3. Both a seeing eye and sunlight are necessary.

The mention of the two examples, "the blind and the seeing" and "darkness and light," seemingly points to the reality that to observe an objective fact, two things are necessary: both a seeing eye and rays of light. If either of these two is absent, observation does not occur. Now one must consider the state of those who are deprived of both: both sight and light. The polytheists are the true manifestation of this; the eye of their intellect is blind, and the darkness of disbelief and idolatry has enveloped their environment. For this reason, they are wandering in bypasses and precipices. In contrast are the believers who, with a truth-seeking vision and a clear program, and by seeking aid from the light of revelation and the teachings of the Prophets, have clearly found their life's path.

4. Does God's Creatorship of all things imply predestination (Jabr)?

A group of proponents of the school of predestination have argued using the phrase "Allah is the Creator of all things" in the above verse to prove their objective, saying that the term "all things" (kullu shay') is so broad that it includes the actions of servants as well; therefore, the creator of our deeds is also God, meaning we have no free will! This statement can be answered in two ways:

First, the other sentences in this verse completely negate this claim, as He severely reproaches and blames the idol-worshippers. If we truly had no choice in our actions, what is the purpose of rebuke and blame? If God willed for them to be idol-worshippers, why does He then reproach them? And why does He argue to guide them and change their path? All of these are evidence that people are free and have choice in selecting their path.

Secondly, Creatorship per se (by essence) in all things is exclusive to God, but this does not contradict our being agents with choice in our actions. This is because our power, our intellect and consciousness, and even our choice and freedom of will are all from Him. Therefore, from one perspective, He is the Creator (of everything, including our actions), and we are agents with choice; these two are in a vertical relationship (one derived from the other), not a horizontal one. He is the Creator of all the means of the action, and we are the users of these means in the path of good or evil.

This is exactly like someone who has prepared a power plant or water plumbing facilities and placed them all at our disposal. Certainly, any use we make of that electricity and this water would not have been possible without his help, yet the final decision rests with us: whether to use that electricity to light an operating room for a dying patient, or for a gathering and center of corruption and pollution; or to use that water to quench the thirst of a thirsty person and grow a flower, or to pour it under the house of an innocent person and destroy his wall.

Ayah

أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌۢ بِقَدَرِهَا فَٱحْتَمَلَ ٱلسَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا ۚ وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ٱبْتِغَآءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَـٰعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَٱلْبَـٰطِلَ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَآءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ (١٧)
He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries along a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for the purpose of [making] ornaments or wares, [there arises] a similar scum. That is how Allah compares truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and that which profits the people stays in the earth. That is how Allah draws comparisons. (17)

Commentary

A Precise Depiction of the Scene of Truth and Falsehood

Since the method of the Quran, as a book of education and upbringing, relies on objective matters, it points to interesting and beautiful sensory parables from people's daily lives to bring complex concepts closer to the mind. Here, too, it presents a very eloquent parable to visualize the truths mentioned in the previous verses regarding monotheism and polytheism, faith and disbelief, and truth and falsehood.

First, It says: "He sends down water from the sky" (أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً).

A life-giving and life-creating water, the source of growth and movement:

"And then the valleys, hollows, and rivers of the earth each receive a portion of this water according to their capacity and extent" (فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها).

Small streams join hands to create rivers; rivers merge, and a great flood pours down from the mountainsides. The waters surge over one another, picking up everything in their path and constantly crashing against each other. At this moment, foam appears from between the waves, just as the Quran says: "The flood carries a swelling foam" (فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً).

"Rabi" (رابى) is derived from the root "rabw" (on the weight of ghuluw), meaning height and superiority. Riba (usury), which means profit, extra money, or additional goods, is also from this same root and carries the same meaning because it denotes addition and excess.

The appearance of foam is not limited to the falling of rain; rather, "in the metals that are melted by fire to make ornaments or tools of life, they also have foam similar to the foam of water" (وَ مِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ) [^1].

[^1]: The literal translation of the above sentence is: "From that which they kindle fire upon to obtain ornaments or goods, there results foam like the foam of water." This expression refers to furnaces for melting metals where fire exists both beneath the metallic materials and above them. This means a layer of fire is at the bottom, then the stones containing sufficient minerals are poured over it, and fire is again placed on top. This is the best type of furnace, where the fire surrounds the meltable materials from all sides.

After presenting this parable in a broad and extensive manner—not only regarding water but also regarding all metals, whether those used for decoration or those from which life's tools are made—It proceeds to draw a conclusion, stating:

"Thus Allah strikes a parable for truth and falsehood" (كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَ الْباطِلَ).

Then He explains it, saying: "As for the foam, it passes away as scum, but as for that which benefits the people, it remains in the earth" (فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَ أَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

The useless, loud-mouthed, and hollow foams that always sit on top but have no merit must be cast aside. But the silent, quiet, humble, useful, and beneficial water remains. Even if it does not stay on the surface, it penetrates the depths of the earth and soon emerges as springs, qanats, and wells, quenching the thirsty, making trees fruitful, flowers bloom, and fruits ripen, bringing order to everything.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis and to invite a more careful study of this parable, He says: "Thus Allah strikes the parables" (كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثالَ).

Key Points

1- The Way to Recognize Truth and Falsehood:

In this meaningful parable, expressed in rhythmic words and phrases, depicting the scene of truth and falsehood in the most sublime form, many truths are hidden, some of which we mention here:

1- Recognizing truth and falsehood—which is the same as distinguishing realities from illusions—sometimes becomes so difficult and complex for man that he must inevitably look for signs and, through those signs, distinguish realities from delusions and truth from falsehood.

The Quran describes these signs in the above parable as follows:

A- Truth is always useful and beneficial, like pure water which is the essence of life. But falsehood is useless and vain; neither does the foam on the water ever quench anyone's thirst or grow a tree, nor can the foam that appears in metal-smelting furnaces be used to make an ornament or a tool for life. Even if they have a use, they are very lowly and worthless uses that do not count, like using dry weeds for burning.

B- Falsehood is always arrogant, sitting on top, loud, full of noise and clamor, yet hollow and devoid of substance. But truth is humble, quiet, a man of action, full of substance, and heavy-weighted. [^1]

C- Truth is always self-reliant, but falsehood seeks help from the prestige of truth and tries to dress itself in its clothing and use its reputation. Just as "every lie takes its brilliance from the truth," for if there were no truthful speech in the world, no one would ever believe a lie; and if there were no genuine goods in the world, no one would be deceived by a counterfeit. Therefore, even the fleeting brilliance of falsehood and its temporary reputation and prestige are by the grace of truth. But truth is everywhere self-reliant, possessing its own reputation and effect!

2- What is "Zabad"?

"Zabad" (زبد) means the foam on top of water or any kind of froth. We know that pure water rarely shows foam on its surface, as foam occurs due to the contamination of water with foreign bodies. From this, it becomes clear that if truth remains in its original clarity and purity, the foams of falsehood will never appear around it. However, when truth takes on the color of the environment due to contact with contaminated environments, and reality becomes mixed with superstition, and right with wrong, or purity with impurity, the foams of falsehood appear alongside it.

This is exactly what Ali عليه السلام pointed to in Nahj al-Balagha when he said:

"If falsehood were pure from the mixture of truth, it would not be hidden from those who seek it. And if truth were pure from the covering of falsehood, the tongues of the stubborn would be cut off from it!" [^1]

Some commentators have said that in the above verse, there are actually three metaphors: the "revelation of the Quranic verses" from the heaven of revelation is likened to the falling of life-giving raindrops; the "hearts of humans" are likened to lands and valleys, each benefiting according to the extent of its existence; and "Satanic whispers" are likened to the contaminated foam on the water. This foam is not produced by the water itself but by the contamination of the place where the water falls. For this reason, the whispers of the self and Satan are not from divine teachings but from the contamination of the human heart. In any case, eventually, these whispers are removed from the hearts of the believers, and the pure water of revelation, which leads to the guidance and life of humans, remains!

3- Benefits are always proportional to readiness and merit!

From this verse, it is also understood that in the source of Divine grace, there is no stinginess, limitation, or prohibition. Just as the clouds in the sky scatter rain everywhere without condition, it is the different pieces of land and valleys that each benefit from it according to the extent of their own existence. A smaller land has a smaller share, and a wider land has a larger share. The same applies to the hearts and souls of humans in the face of God's grace!

4- Falsehood seeks a chaotic market!

When the flood enters a flat plain and the boiling and roaring of the water subsides, the objects mixed with the water gradually settle, the foams disappear, and the pure water reveals its true face. Similarly, falsehood seeks a chaotic market to exploit it. But when tranquility is found, and everyone sits in their proper place, and criteria and standards become clear in society, falsehood finds no place for itself and quickly moves aside!

5- Falsehood does not appear in only one garment.

One of the characteristics of falsehood is that it "appears in one form at one moment and in another garment the next," so that if it is recognized in one garment, it can hide its face in another. In the above verse, there is a subtle reference to this issue where it says: foam does not only appear on water; in every furnace and every crucible where metals are melted, new foams appear in a new shape and a new garment. In other words, truth and falsehood exist everywhere, just as foam appears in every liquid in its appropriate form.

Therefore, we must never be deceived by the variety of forms and should expect to recognize falsehood everywhere by its specific traits—which are the same everywhere and were mentioned above—and cast them aside.

6- The survival of every being depends on the extent of its benefit!

In the above verse, we read that what benefits people remains and stays:

(وَ أَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ).

Not only does water, which is the essence of life, remain while the foams perish, but also in metals—whether those for "hilyah" (ornament) or those for preparing "mata'" (goods) and tools of life—the pure metal, which is useful and beneficial or transparent and beautiful, remains, and the foams are cast away.

Similarly, humans, groups, schools of thought, and programs have the right to survive and live only to the extent that they are useful and beneficial. If we see a false school of thought remaining standing for a while, it is because of that amount of truth mixed into it, which has given it a proportional right to exist!

7- How Truth ejects Falsehood.

The word "jufa'" (جفاء), which means being thrown out or jumping out, contains a subtle point: falsehood reaches a point where it lacks the power to maintain itself, and at that moment, it is thrown out from the core of society. This happens exactly when truth begins to boil. When truth surges, falsehood is cast out like the foam on a boiling pot. This itself is proof that truth must always boil and surge to distance falsehood from itself!

8- Falsehood is indebted to Truth for its survival.

As we said in the commentary of the verse, if there were no water, foam could never continue its life independently. Likewise, if there were no truth, falsehood would have no brilliance. If there were no righteous individuals, no one would be influenced by traitors or deceived by them. Thus, this very maneuvering and false brilliance of falsehood is indebted to exploiting the brilliance of truth (Indeed, the lie takes its brilliance from the truth!).

9- The struggle between Truth and Falsehood is eternal.

The Quran here presents a parable to visualize truth and falsehood that is not specific to a certain time or place. it is a scene visualized before human eyes every year in different parts of the world. This shows that the battle between truth and falsehood is not a temporary or local battle. These veins of sweet and salty water will flow over creation until the blowing of the Trumpet, except for the time when the world and humanity become an ideal society (like the society of the era of the uprising of Mahdi عليه السلام), when the end of this struggle will be announced, the army of truth will be victorious, the carpet of falsehood will be rolled up, and humanity will enter a new stage of its history. Until this historical stage arrives, one must expect the clash of truth and falsehood everywhere and maintain the necessary stance against falsehood.

10- Life in the light of effort and Jihad.

The beautiful parable above also clarifies this fundamental principle of human life: that life without Jihad, and survival and honor without effort, are impossible. For it says that what people send into the furnaces to prepare the means of life (necessities) or ornaments (comforts of life) always has scum and foam. To obtain these two (necessities and comforts—ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ), the raw materials, which are not found pure in nature and are always mixed with other things, must be placed under the pressure of fire in the furnace and refined and purified so that the pure and clean metal emerges. This work is not accomplished except in the shadow of effort, endeavor, and struggle.

Fundamentally, the nature of worldly life is such that alongside flowers are thorns, alongside sweetness are stings, and victories are found within the folds of hardships and problems. It has been said since ancient times: "Treasures are in ruins, and atop every treasure sleeps a dangerous dragon." Are those ruins and this dragon anything other than the mass of problems that exist in achieving any success?

In our own Iranian stories, Rostam had to pass through seven trials (Haft Khan) to reach victory, each referring to a type of the mass of problems in the path of any positive activity.

In any case, the Quran has repeatedly expressed this truth in various phrases: that without enduring suffering, man achieves no success.

In verse 214 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we read: "Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you? They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, 'When is the help of Allah?' Unquestionably, the help of Allah is near."

In these very difficult and painful moments, Divine help came to them, and they were told: the victory of Allah is near!

The Parables of the Quran.

The role of parables in explaining and interpreting topics is undeniable. For this reason, in no science are we independent of mentioning parables to prove truths and to clarify and bring them closer to the mind. Sometimes, a well-placed parable that is perfectly aligned and consistent with the objective brings the matter down from the heavens to the earth and makes it understandable for everyone.

Altogether, it can be said that parables in various scientific, educational, social, moral, and other fields have the following effective roles:

1- Parables make issues sensory—

Since humans are more accustomed to sensory things and complex intellectual truths are further from the reach of relatively common thoughts, sensory parables bring them from a distant range to a close one, placing them on the threshold of the senses, making their understanding pleasant, sweet, and reassuring.

2- Parables shorten the path—

Sometimes, to prove a deep logical and intellectual issue, one must resort to various arguments that are still surrounded by ambiguity. But mentioning a clear parable that is perfectly aligned with the objective shortens the path so much that it increases the impact of the arguments and reduces the need for multiple proofs.

3- Parables make issues universal—

There are many scientific topics that, in their original form, are only understandable to the elite, and the masses do not benefit much from them. But when mixed with parables and thus made understandable, people at any level of science and knowledge benefit from them. Therefore, parables have an undeniable application as a means of generalizing science and culture.

4- Parables increase the level of certainty in issues—

Intellectual universals, no matter how well-reasoned and logical, do not create sufficient certainty as long as they are only in the mind, because man always seeks certainty in objectivity. Parables give objectivity to mental issues and clarify their application in the external world, and for this reason, they affect the level of belief, acceptance, and certainty regarding an issue.

5- Parables silence the stubborn—

It often happens that mentioning the generalities of issues in a reasoned and logical way is not enough to silence a stubborn person, and he continues to struggle. But when the issue is cast into the mold of a parable, it closes the path for him in such a way that no room for excuse-making remains.

It is not bad to present "parables" for this very subject! So that it becomes clear how effective the role of parables is.

In the Holy Quran, we read that in response to those who objected to the creation of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام only from a mother—asking how it is possible for a human to be born without a father—He says: "Indeed, the likeness of Jesus near Allah is as the likeness of Adam; He created him from dust" (Al-Imran: 59).

Observe carefully: no matter how much we want to tell stubborn individuals that this is a simple matter before the infinite power of God, they may still make excuses. But when we say to them: Do you accept that Adam, the first human, was created from dust? How can the God who has such power not be able to cause a human to be born from a human being without a father?

And regarding the hypocrites who live a seemingly peaceful life for a few days in the shadow of their hypocrisy, the Holy Quran has a beautiful parable, likening them to a traveler caught in a dark desert on a gloomy night, trapped by thunder, lightning, storm, and rain. He is so bewildered that he has no way out. Only when the lightning appears does the desert space brighten for a few moments, and he decides to move in a direction to perhaps find the way, but the lightning quickly goes out, and he remains just as bewildered! Can there be a more interesting expression to depict the state of a bewildered hypocrite who wants to continue his life by using the spirit of hypocrisy and hypocritical action? (Surah Al-Baqarah: 20).

Or when we tell people to spend in the way of Allah, and that Allah rewards your spending many times over, ordinary people might not fully grasp the concept of this statement. But when it is said that spending is like a seed sown in the ground from which seven ears grow, and in each ear there may be a hundred grains, the issue becomes completely understandable: "The example of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is like a seed [of grain] which grows seven spikes; in each spike is a hundred grains" (Al-Baqarah: 261).

We often say that ostentatious deeds are useless and no result comes to man from them. This statement might seem heavy to some—how can a beneficial act like building a hospital or a school, even if for the purpose of showing off and hypocrisy, be worthless before God? But the Quran, by mentioning a parable, makes it completely palatable and understandable, saying: "His example is like that of a [large] smooth stone upon which is some dust and is hit by a downpour that leaves it bare":

"The deed of such people is like a piece of stone upon which some dust has been poured and a seed sown in it. When it rains, instead of this seed becoming fruitful, it washes it away along with the surface dust on the stone and casts it aside. Such is the state of ostentatious and rootless deeds!" (Al-Baqarah: 264).

Let us not go far; in this very parable under discussion regarding the struggle between truth and falsehood, how well this issue is visualized and precisely depicted! The premises, the results, the traits, characteristics, and effects of each of truth and falsehood are reflected in this parable, such that it is understandable and reassuring for everyone, silencing for the stubborn, and above all, it removes the burden of long and detailed discussions.

In one of the narrations, we read that one of the materialists (Zanadiqa) came to Imam Sadiq عليه السلام and said: God says, "Every time their skins are roasted [by the fire], We will replace them with other skins so they may taste the punishment." What is the sin of that "other" skin that it too should be punished?! The Imam said: That skin is both the same as the first skin and other than it. The questioner was not convinced and could not understand anything from this answer, but the Imam عليه السلام, by mentioning an eloquent parable, clarified the issue so much that no room for discussion remained. He said:

Observe: you crush a decayed and worn-out brick, then you make clay from that same dust, put it in a mold, and make a new brick from it. This is the same as the first brick, yet from one perspective, it is other than it. [[^1]]

But here, mentioning one point is absolutely necessary: a parable, with all its valuable and effective roles, can only perform its fundamental role if it is completely consistent and harmonious with the subject for which the parable was chosen. Otherwise, it will be misleading and destructive. That is, to the same extent that a correct and harmonious parable is useful and effective, a deviant parable is destructive, ruinous, and misleading.

For this reason, hypocrites and ill-wishers always use deviant parables to mislead people and deceive the simple-minded, using the brilliance of a parable to support their lies. We must carefully watch out for such deviant parables and the misuse of parables.

[^1]: Read the explanation of this Hadith in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 428, which we have quoted from the Majalis of Shaykh and the Ihtijaj of Tabarsi.

Ayah

لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُۥ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لَٱفْتَدَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْحِسَابِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٨)
For those who answer [the summons of] their Lord there shall be the best [of rewards]. But those who do not answer Him, even if they possessed all that is on the earth and as much of it besides, they would surely offer it to redeem themselves with it. For such there shall be an adverse reckoning, and their refuge shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place. (18)

Commentary

Those Who Responded to the Call of Truth

After illustrating the nature of Truth and Falsehood through an eloquent and profound parable in the preceding verses, the text now points to the destiny of those who responded to the call of Truth and believed in it, as well as those who turned away from the Truth and inclined toward Falsehood.

First, It says: "For those who responded to the call of their Lord is the best reward [and a fruitful result and a praised end]" (لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِرَبِّهِمُ الْحُسْنىٰ). The word "Husna" (the best/goodness) has a broad meaning that encompasses every good and felicity, ranging from noble traits and moral virtues to a pure and wholesome social life, victory over enemies, and the eternal Paradise.

Then It adds: "And those who did not respond to Him—their fate is so ominous and pitiable that if they possessed all that is in the earth and the like of it with it, they would [attempt to] ransom themselves with it," yet it will not be accepted from them (وَ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً وَ مِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لَافْتَدَوْا بِهِ).

To depict the magnitude of their punishment and retribution, there is no expression more eloquent than this: that a human being should own the entire earth, rather twice its extent, and give it all to save themselves from that grim fate, yet it would be of no avail.

This sentence, in reality, points to the fact that the ultimate desire of a human being, beyond which nothing can be imagined, is to own the entire earth. However, the severity of the punishment for the oppressors and those who oppose the call of Truth is at such a level that they would be willing to sacrifice this ultimate worldly goal—and even more—just to be set free. Furthermore, even if it were accepted from them, it would only be a rescue from punishment, while there would be no sign of the extraordinarily great rewards given to those who responded to the call of Truth.

From this, it becomes clear that the expression "and the like of it with it" (وَ مِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ) does not merely mean adding another equivalent of the entire globe; rather, it means that no matter how much more they might possess, they would be willing to give it for their liberation. The reason is clear: a human wants everything for himself; when he himself is drowning in torment, what benefit does the ownership of the entire world bring him?

Following this misery (the non-acceptance of all that is on earth in exchange for his salvation), It points to another of their misfortunes, saying: "Those will have the worst reckoning" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْحسابِ).

Regarding what is meant by "the worst reckoning" (Su’ al-Hisab), commentators have offered various interpretations. Some believe it refers to a meticulous accounting without the slightest leniency, for "Su’ al-Hisab" in the sense of injustice or oppression has no meaning regarding the Just God.

A hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام also confirms this interpretation. In this hadith, we read that the Imam said to one of his companions: "Why does so-and-so complain about you?" He replied: "His complaint is because I took my right from him to the very last bit." When the Imam heard this, he sat up in an angry state and then said:

"It is as if you think that if you demand your right to the utmost limit, you have not done evil. Have you not seen what Allah عز و جل related: 'And they fear the worst reckoning' (وَ يَخافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسابِ)? Do you think they fear that Allah will be unjust to them? No, by Allah, they do not fear except the meticulousness [of the accounting], so Allah عز و جل named it 'the worst reckoning.' Therefore, whoever is overly meticulous and harsh in settling an account has indeed committed 'bad accounting' (Su’ al-Hisab)." [^1]

Others have said that "the worst reckoning" means that their accounting is accompanied by reproach, rebuke, and the like, which causes them suffering in addition to the terror of the accounting itself.

Others still have said that "the worst reckoning" means "the worst retribution," meaning there is an evil reward for them. This is exactly like when we say "so-and-so's account is clean" or "so-and-so's account is dark and murky," meaning the result of their accounting is good or bad. Or when we say "Give him his account in the palm of his hand!", meaning punish him according to his deeds.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 288 (Although this hadith is mentioned in the commentary of verse 21 of this Surah, it is clear that it defines the term "Su’ al-Hisab" generally and for all instances).

These three interpretations are not mutually exclusive, and it is possible that all of them are intended by the verse. That is, such individuals will undergo a harsh and meticulous accounting, will be rebuked and reproached during the process, and their punishment after the reckoning will be given to them in full without any reduction.

At the end of the verse, It points to the third punishment or the final result of their retribution, saying: "And their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the resting place" (وَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ بِئْسَ الْمِهادُ).

"Mihad" is originally derived from the root "Mahd," meaning to prepare and make ready. It is also used to mean a bed or cradle, which a person uses for rest and tranquility, because it is prepared and made ready for resting. The mention of this word is an allusion to the fact that these transgressors, instead of reclining in a bed of rest, must lie upon the flames of a scorching fire!

Key Point

It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that people on the Day of Resurrection will be divided into two groups. There is a group whose account Allah will settle with ease and simplicity, and He will not exercise any harshness toward them: "Then as for he who is given his record in his right hand, he will be judged with an easy reckoning" (Surah Al-Inshiqaq, 8). Conversely, there are those whose account will be settled with "severity," and every atom and weight will be scrutinized, as He says regarding certain lands whose people were rebellious and sinful: "Then We settled its account with a severe reckoning and punished it with a horrific punishment" (At-Talaq, 8), or in the verse under discussion where the expression "the worst reckoning" is used.

This is because some people, in this worldly life and in settling accounts with others, are extraordinarily harsh and, as the saying goes, "pull a hair out of the yogurt" (are excessively meticulous). They strive to take their right to the last penny at any cost, and when a mistake is made by another, they punish him to the maximum extent possible. These are people who, in life, have the least amount of forgiveness, even toward their spouse, children, brothers, and friends. Since the life of the next world is a reflection and echo of the life in this world, Allah will also be so strict in their accounting that they must answer for every deed they have done without the slightest leniency being shown to them.

But on the contrary, there are those who are easy-going, forgiving, and magnanimous, especially when they deal with a friend, an acquaintance, those who have a right over them, or the weak. They are so noble that they try in many cases to overlook things and ignore the faults of others—specifically faults that are personal in nature toward them. Allah will be easy with such people and will include them in His infinite forgiveness and vast mercy, accounting for them with an "easy reckoning." This is a great lesson for all human beings, especially those who hold positions of authority and deal with many people.

Ayahs

۞ أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩)
Is someone who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, like someone who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition (19)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ ٱلْمِيثَـٰقَ (٢٠)
—those who fulfill Allah’s covenant and do not break the pledge solemnly made, (20)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَيَخَافُونَ سُوٓءَ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢١)
and those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined, and fear their Lord, and are afraid of an adverse reckoning (21)
وَٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٢٢)
—those who are patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, maintain the prayer, and spend secretly and openly out of what We have provided them, and repel evil [conduct] with good. For such will be the reward of the [ultimate] abode: (22)
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن كُلِّ بَابٍ (٢٣)
the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter along with whoever is righteous from among their forebears, their spouses, and their descendants, and the angels will call on them from every door: (23)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُم بِمَا صَبَرْتُمْ ۚ فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٢٤)
‘Peace be to you, for your patience.’ How excellent is the reward of the [ultimate] abode! (24)

Commentary

The Eight Gates of Heaven and the Program of the People of Understanding (Ulul-Albab).

In the verses under discussion, a depiction and visualization of the constructive programs of the followers of Truth are presented, which complete the discussion of the preceding verses.

In the first verse under discussion, it asks in the form of a rhetorical question: "Is he who knows that what has been revealed to you from your Lord is the Truth like him who is blind?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَعْلَمُ أَنَّما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ الْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمى‌).

What a fascinating expression! It does not say: "Is he who knows that this Quran is the Truth like him who does not know?" Rather, it says: "Is he who knows this like one who is blind?" This expression is a subtle allusion to the fact that not knowing this reality is impossible unless the eye of the human heart has completely ceased to function; otherwise, how could a person with healthy, seeing eyes fail to see the face of the sun? And the greatness of this Quran is exactly like the light of the sun.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it adds: "Only those of understanding (Ulul-Albab) take heed." (إِنَّما يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ‌).

"Albab" is the plural of "Lubb," meaning the "core" or "pith" of anything. Thus, the opposite of "Ulul-Albab" are those who are brainless, hollow, and devoid of substance.

According to some great commentators, this verse, with the utmost emphasis, invites people to acquire knowledge and combat ignorance, as it considers those lacking knowledge to be blind.

Then, as a commentary on "Ulul-Albab," it details the sub-programs of the followers of Truth. Before anything else, it points to the issue of fulfilling covenants and abandoning the breaking of promises, saying: "They are those who fulfill the covenant of Allah and do not break the pledge" (الَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَ لا يَنْقُضُونَ الْمِيثاقَ‌).

There is no doubt that "the covenant of Allah" (Ahd-Allah) has a broad meaning: it includes innate (fitrī) covenants and pledges that God has taken from man by virtue of his nature (such as the nature of monotheism and the love for truth and justice), rational pledges (meaning the truths of existence, the Origin, and the Resurrection that man perceives through the power of thought and reason), and legislative (shar'ī) pledges (meaning what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took from the believers regarding obedience to Allah's commands and the abandonment of sin).

Naturally, fulfilling the pledges that a human makes with other humans is also included in this set, because Allah has commanded that these pledges also be respected; rather, they are included in both legislative and rational pledges.

The second item of their program is the preservation of ties and guarding them, as He says: "And those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ ما أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَنْ يُوصَلَ‌). A broader expression than this cannot be found in this context, for man has a connection with Allah, a connection with the Prophets and leaders, a connection with other humans—including friends, neighbors, relatives, religious brothers, and fellow human beings—and also a connection with himself. The above instruction says all these ties must be respected, the rights of all must be fulfilled, and no action should be taken that leads to the severance of any of these ties.

In reality, man is not an isolated being, detached and cut off from the universe; rather, his entire existence is composed of ties, interests, and connections.

On one hand, he has a connection with the Creator of this system; if he cuts his connection with Him, he will perish, just as the light of a lamp perishes when its connection to the source of electricity is cut. Therefore, just as he has a creative (takwīnī) connection with this Great Source, he must also establish his connection in terms of obedience to command and legislation (tashrī').

On the other hand, he has a connection with the Prophet and the Imam as a leader and guide, the severance of which leaves him wandering in the wilderness.

Thirdly, he has a connection with the entire human society, especially those who have more rights over him, such as parents, relatives, friends, teachers, and mentors.

And fourthly, he has a connection with his own self, in the sense that he is commanded to preserve his own interests and his growth and perfection.

Establishing each of these ties is, in fact, an instance of "joining what Allah has commanded to be joined," and severing any of these ties is severing "what Allah has commanded to be joined," because Allah has ordered the joining of all these ties.

From what we have said, the interpretation of the hadiths narrated regarding this verse becomes clear. Sometimes it is interpreted as relatives and kin, sometimes as the Imam and leader of the religion, sometimes as the Family of Muhammad (Ahl al-Bayt), and sometimes as all people of faith! For example, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he was asked about the interpretation of "those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined," and he said: "It refers to your relatives." [^1] Also, in another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read that he said: "It was revealed...

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 494.

...regarding the kinship of the Family of Muhammad, and it may also apply to your own relatives."

Interestingly, at the end of this hadith, we read that the Imam said: "Do not be of those who say a thing is limited to only one thing." [^1] This sentence is a clear indication of the breadth of the meanings of the Quranic verses, which we have repeatedly pointed out. Again, in a third hadith from that same great leader, we read that in interpreting this verse, he said: "It is the joining with the Imam in every year with what is little or much," then he said: "And I do not intend by that except your purification." [^2]

The third and fourth programs of the followers of Truth are that "they fear their Lord and dread the evil of the reckoning in the court of Resurrection" (وَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَ يَخافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسابِ‌).

Regarding the difference between "khashyah" (dread) and "khawf" (fear), although both are close to each other, some have said: Khashyah is fear accompanied by respect for the other party and accompanied by knowledge and certainty; hence, in the Quran, this state is specifically attributed to the scholars: "Only those of His servants who possess knowledge fear Allah" (Fatir: 28).

However, considering the usage of the word "khashyah" in the Quran, which is used in many verses, it becomes clear that it is used in the exact same sense as "khawf" and is used synonymously with it.

Here a question arises: Is the fear of the Lord anything other than the fear of His reckoning and punishment? If so, what is the difference between "fearing their Lord" and "dreading the evil of the reckoning"?

The answer is that fear of Allah is not necessarily always in the sense of fear of His punishment and reckoning; rather, the greatness of His station and the feeling of the weight of the duty of servitude (even without regard for punishment and penalty) automatically creates a kind of state of fear and awe in the hearts of the people of faith—a fear born of faith and awareness of Allah's greatness and the sense of responsibility toward Him (Verse 28 of Fatir may refer to this very meaning).

Another question that arises here is regarding the "evil of the reckoning" (sū' al-hisāb). Is there truly "bad accounting" in the Resurrection when people's deeds are calculated?! We explained the answer to this question a few verses prior where this same word was used, and we said: It refers to a meticulous audit without leniency, calculating all details—metaphorically, "pulling a hair out of the yogurt" (extreme precision). An interesting hadith was also mentioned there in this regard.

And as we said there, it is also possible that "evil of the reckoning" refers to an accounting accompanied by reproach and rebuke. Some have interpreted "evil of the reckoning" as "evil of the recompense," meaning a bad reward, just as we say "give someone their account in the palm of their hand," meaning punish them.

We also suggested the possibility that "evil of the reckoning" has a comprehensive concept that includes all these meanings.

The "fifth" program of theirs is steadfastness (patience) in the face of all the difficulties that exist in the path of obedience, the abandonment of sin, Jihad against the enemy, and the struggle against oppression and corruption [^1]. Moreover, it is a patience and steadfastness for the sake of seeking the pleasure of the Lord; hence, He says: "And those who are patient, seeking the countenance of their Lord" (وَ الَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ‌).

We have repeatedly mentioned the concept of "Sabr," which is "steadfastness in the broad sense of the word." As for the expression "the countenance of their Lord" (wajhi rabbihim), it conveys one of two meanings:

First, "wajh" (face/countenance) in such cases means "greatness," just as a significant "view" or "opinion" is called "hadha wajh al-ra'y" (this is a significant opinion). This is perhaps because "wajh" originally means the face, and the human face is the most important external part of the body, as the important organs of speech, sight, and hearing are located in it.

Second, "the countenance of the Lord" here means "the pleasure and satisfaction of the Lord," meaning they exercise patience and steadfastness in the face of all difficulties for the sake of seeking the pleasure of the Truth. The use of "wajh" in this sense is because when a person wants to seek someone's pleasure, he turns his face toward them (thus, "wajh" here has a metaphorical aspect).

In any case, this sentence is clear evidence that patience and endurance, and generally any good deed, is valuable only if it is "seeking the countenance of Allah" and for Allah. If it is done for other motives, such as hypocrisy (riya') and attracting people's attention—that he is a steadfast and virtuous person—or even to satisfy his own pride, it is worthless.

As some commentators have said, sometimes a person is patient in the face of unpleasant events so that people will say how steadfast he is; sometimes out of fear that they might say what a person of low capacity he is; sometimes so that enemies do not gloat over him; sometimes because he knows that restlessness and lamentation are futile; and sometimes to take on the appearance of being oppressed so that others rise to help him. None of these things are evidence of the perfection of the human soul.

But when he exercises patience and steadfastness for the sake of obeying Allah's command and because every event that occurs in life has a reason and wisdom—in such a way that he breaks the greatness of the event and refrains from opening his tongue in ingratitude or that which is evidence of lamentation—this is the very patience referred to in the above verse and is considered "seeking the countenance of Allah."

The "sixth" program of theirs is that "they establish prayer" (وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ).

Although establishing prayer is one of the instances of fulfilling the covenant of Allah, and even a living instance of preserving divine ties, and from one perspective an instance of patience and steadfastness, since these general concepts have some very important specific instances that are extraordinarily effective in human destiny, they have been specifically pointed out in this and the following sentences.

What is more important than for a person to renew his relationship and connection with Allah every morning and evening, to rise in intimate prayer with Him, to remember His greatness and his own responsibilities, and in the light of this act, to wash the dust and rust of sin from his heart and soul, and to gain substance by joining the drop of his existence to the boundless ocean of the Truth of existence? Yes, prayer has all these blessings and effects.

[^1]: Patience is necessary not only in the face of "obedience," "sin," and "calamity," but one must also be patient in the face of "blessings," meaning that they should not lead a person to pride, intoxication, and lack of restraint.

And following that, He describes the "seventh" program of the seekers of Truth with this phrase: "They are those who spend from what We have provided them, secretly and openly" (وَ أَنْفَقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ سِرًّا وَ عَلانِيَةً).

It is not only in this verse that the issue of spending (infaq) or Zakat follows prayer; in many verses of the Quran, this sequence is seen, because one strengthens the human's connection with "Allah" and the other strengthens his connection with "creation."

Here, attention to the phrase "from what We have provided them" (mimmā razaqnāhum) is necessary, as it includes every kind of blessing, whether wealth, knowledge, power, social status, influence, or otherwise; for spending should not be one-dimensional but should occur in all dimensions and all blessings.

The expression "secretly and openly" (sirran wa 'alāniyatan) is another indication of the fact that in their spending, they also consider the manner of it. Sometimes, if spending is done secretly, it is much more effective, and this is in cases where preserving the dignity of the recipient requires it, or regarding the spender, so that he is immune from hypocrisy and showing off his deed to others. And sometimes, if it is done openly, its effect will be broader and greater, and this is in cases where it encourages others to this good deed and leads to following his example, such that one good deed of his becomes the cause of tens, hundreds, or thousands of similar good deeds.

From this, it becomes clear that the Quran is so meticulous in performing a positive act that it does not only look at the essence of the work but emphasizes that both the essence of the deed must be good and the quality of its performance (in cases where a task can be performed with different qualities).

Finally, the "eighth" and last program of theirs is that "they repel evil with good" (وَ يَدْرَؤُنَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ السَّيِّئَةَ).

This means that upon committing a sin or a slip, they do not suffice with merely regretting, repenting, and seeking forgiveness; rather, they practically rise to compensate. The greater their sin or slip, the more good deeds they perform, so that they may wash their existence and society from the filth of sin with the water of good deeds.

"Yadra'ūn" is from the root "dar'" (rhyming with zar'), meaning to repel or push away.

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that they do not retaliate evil with evil; rather, they try, if evil reaches them from someone, to make him ashamed and force him to reconsider by doing good toward him, just as we read in verse 34 of Surah Fussilat: "Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better; and thereupon the one whom between you and him is enmity [will become] as though he was a devoted friend."

At the same time, there is no obstacle to the verse under discussion intending to express both meanings.

In Islamic traditions, hadiths have also been narrated with the content of both interpretations.

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said to Mu'adh ibn Jabal: "When you have done an evil deed, do a good deed beside it to erase it." [^1] And in Nahj al-Balagha, we read that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: "Reprove your brother by being kind to him, and turn away his evil by bestowing favors upon him." [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 158.

Of course, it must be noted that this is a moral ruling and is specific to cases where such programs are effective; otherwise, the implementation of legal punishments (hudūd) and the penalizing of wrongdoers, which is established as a law in Islamic legislation, is applied equally to all who are subject to it.

At the end of the verse, after mentioning the various past programs, He refers to the outcome of the work of the "Ulul-Albab," the thinkers, the followers of Truth, and the practitioners of these programs, saying: "For such is the final attainment of the [eternal] home" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ) [^1].

The next verse is an explanation for this good end and favorable outcome, saying: "Their end is the Gardens of Eternity (Eden) which they shall enter, along with the righteous among their fathers, their spouses, and their offspring" (جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَها وَ مَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَ أَزْواجِهِمْ وَ ذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ‌).

And that which completes these great and endless blessings is that "the angels shall enter upon them from every gate" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ‌).

And they say to them: "Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured" (سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِما صَبَرْتُمْ‌). It was those patiences and steadfastnesses of yours in the path of performing duties and responsibilities and enduring hardships and calamities that brought about this peace. Here you will be in ultimate security and tranquility; no war or conflict, no violence or dispute—everywhere is safe and secure, and everything smiles upon you, and here is tranquility free from any mental distress!

And in the end, He says: "How excellent is the final home!" (فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ).

Key Points

1- Why is only patience mentioned?

The phrase "Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured" says that the angels' greeting to the people of Paradise is such that they say: "Peace be upon you because of your patience and steadfastness," while in the above verses, eight parts of their good deeds and important programs were mentioned, yet in this phrase, only one of those eight programs is pointed out, and that is patience.

The point of this subject can be understood from the vivid and profound statement of Ali عليه السلام: "Patience is to faith as the head is to the body; there is no good in a body that has no head, and no good in faith that has no patience." [^1] In reality, the backbone of all constructive individual and social programs is that very endurance and steadfastness. If it were not there, none of them would reach fruition, because in the path of every positive work, there are difficulties and obstacles that cannot be overcome except with the power of steadfastness. Neither the fulfillment of a covenant is possible without steadfastness and patience, nor the preservation of divine ties, nor the fear of Allah and the court of Resurrection, nor the establishment of prayer and spending from divine blessings, nor the compensation of errors with good deeds!

[^1]: "Uqba" means the consequence and end of a matter, whether good or bad, but given the context of the situation and the speech, the intended meaning in the above verse is the good end. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 82.

2- The Gates of Paradise.

It is well understood from both the verses of the Quran and the narrations that Paradise has gates. However, this multiplicity of gates is not because the number of those entering Paradise is such that if they wanted to enter through one gate it would cause trouble, nor is it because there is a kind of group or class difference where each group is obliged to enter through one gate, nor is it for the proximity or distance of the path, nor for beauty and variety due to the abundance of gates! Fundamentally, the gates of Paradise are not like the gates of this world that are placed at the entrance of gardens, palaces, and houses.

Rather, these gates refer to the deeds and actions that cause entry into Paradise. Therefore, we read in some reports that "Paradise has gates with different names, including: it has a gate called 'Bab al-Mujahidin' (The Gate of the Warriors)! And the warriors, armed with the same weapons with which they fought, enter Paradise through that gate, and the angels welcome them!" [^1] We read in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "Know that Paradise has eight gates, the width of each gate of which is the distance of forty years' travel!" [^2] This itself shows that "gate" in such cases has a broader concept than what is said in daily expressions.

And interestingly, we read in the Quran that Hell has "seven" gates (لَها سَبْعَةُ أَبْوابٍ‌) (Al-Hijr: 44), and according to narrations, Paradise has "eight" gates, indicating that the ways to reach happiness and the eternal Paradise are more than the ways to reach Hell, and Allah's mercy precedes His wrath: "O He whose mercy precedes His wrath" (Dua Jawshan al-Kabir).

More interestingly, regarding the programs of the "Ulul-Albab" in the above verses, eight programs were also mentioned, each of which is in reality one of the gates of Paradise and a way to reach eternal happiness.

3- Relatives of the People of Paradise join them.

Not only the above verse but some other verses of the Quran also explicitly state that among the assembly of the people of Paradise, their fathers, spouses, and children who were righteous shall enter. This is, in fact, to complete the divine blessings upon them, so that they have no deficiency even in terms of separation from the people they love. Since in that world, which is a new and perfected world, everything becomes fresh and new, they too enter with fresh and new faces and a more intense and warmer love and sincerity—a love that multiplies the value of the heavenly blessings.

Although in the above verse only fathers, children, and spouses are mentioned, in reality, all relatives are gathered in this assembly, because the presence of children and fathers is not possible without the presence of brothers and sisters and even other relatives. This point becomes clear with a little reflection, for whenever someone is a person of Paradise, his righteous father also joins him, and since the righteous father is a person of Paradise, all his children join him, and in this way, brothers are joined together. By the same calculation, other relatives will also be present in that assembly (reflect on this).

4- What is Jannat 'Adn?

"Jannat" means gardens and "'Adn" means "long stay," and here it means eternity. The reason a "mine" is called "ma'dan" is because of the long stay of materials in that place.

It is understood from various verses of the Quran that Paradise is an eternal and everlasting home for its inhabitants. However, as we said under verse 72 of Surah At-Tawbah, it appears from some Quranic verses that "Jannat 'Adn" is a specific place in Paradise that has superiority over other gardens of Paradise, and only three groups reside there: "the Prophets," "the Truthful" (Siddiqin)—meaning their special companions—and "the Martyrs." [^3]

5- Washing away the effects of sin.

There is no doubt that "good deeds" (hasanat) and "evil deeds" (sayyi'at) generally have a reciprocal effect on each other, and we even find examples of this in our daily lives. Sometimes it happens that a person works for many years and, by enduring great hardship, amasses a fortune, but with one act of ignorance, or passion, or carelessness, he loses it. This is nothing other than the destruction of previous material "good deeds," which is referred to in the Quran as "habt" (nullification).

And sometimes, conversely, a person commits many mistakes and carries the burden of heavy losses because of them, but with one wise and calculated act, or a courageous struggle, he compensates for all of them. For example, in the course of this Islamic Revolution that we faced in our own era, we saw people who had committed many sins in the previous oppressive and tyrannical regime and were in prison for that very reason, but when war broke out with the enemies of the country, they were invited to the field because of their military expertise. They, with unique courage and self-sacrifice, dealt fatal blows to the body of the enemy; some were martyred, and some remained, and in both cases, they compensated for their past.

In the verses under discussion, which say: "And they repel evil with good" (the believers of understanding repel their evil deeds with good deeds), this same point is referred to.

For a non-infallible human being will inevitably fall into slips, but the important thing is that afterward, he thinks of compensation. Not only should he wash away the social effects of sin with his good deeds, but he should also clean that darkness and gloom that settles on the human heart and soul from sin with "good deeds" and return it to its innate luminosity and purity.

This act is called in the language of the Quran "takfir" (covering/expiation) and purification (we have given other explanations in this regard in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 69 onwards).

Of course, as we said in the commentary of the above verses, the phrase "and they repel with good" may refer to another important moral virtue, which is that the "Ulul-Albab" do not respond to the evil of others with evil, and instead of taking revenge, they do good, so that the other party becomes ashamed and embarrassed and returns toward purity and reforms himself.

[^1]: Minhaj al-Bara'ah fi Sharh Nahj al-Balagha, Vol. 3, p. 995. [^2]: Al-Khisal by Saduq, Chapters of Eight. [^3]: For further explanation, refer to Vol. 8, p. 38.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ (٢٥)
But as for those who break Allah’s compact after having pledged it solemnly, and sever what Allah has commanded to be joined, and cause corruption in the earth—it is such on whom the curse will lie, and for them will be the ills of the [ultimate] abode. (25)
ٱللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعٌ (٢٦)
Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it. They boast of the life of this world, but compared with the Hereafter the life of this world is but a [trifling] enjoyment. (26)

Commentary

Wicked World-Worshippers!

Since good and evil are always clearly distinguished by comparing one with the other, after mentioning the qualities of the "People of Understanding" (Ulul-Albab) and the seekers of truth—which were detailed in the preceding verses—this portion of the verses describes some of the primary characteristics of the mischief-makers and those who have lost their true reason and intellect. It says:

"And those who break the covenant of Allah after its confirmation, and sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined, and spread corruption on earth—for them is the curse, and for them is the ill abode" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ مِيثاقِهِ وَ يَقْطَعُونَ ما أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَنْ يُوصَلَ وَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ).

In reality, all of their ideological and practical corruptions are summarized in the three phrases above:

1- Breaking Divine covenants, which include primordial (fitrah) covenants, rational covenants, and legislative (shari'a) covenants.

2- Severing ties: the bond with Allah, the bond with Divine leaders, the bond with creation, and the bond with one's own self.

3- The final part, which is the fruit of the previous two, is spreading corruption on earth.

Does one who breaks Allah's covenants and severs ties from every direction do anything other than spread corruption?! These efforts and struggles are carried out by this group to achieve material or even imaginary goals; yet, instead of bringing them closer to a worthy objective, it distances them, for "curse" (la'nah) means being distanced from the mercy of Allah. [^1] It is interesting that in this verse and the preceding one, "the abode" (al-dar) is mentioned in an absolute sense, hinting that the true abode is indeed the Hereafter, for every other abode "will surely suffer decay"!

In the next verse, it points out that sustenance, and its increase or decrease, is in the hand of Allah:

[^1]: Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "La'n" means expulsion and distancing accompanied by wrath. When attributed to Allah in the Hereafter, it refers to punishment and retribution; in this world, it means being cut off from receiving mercy. If it comes from people, it means imprecation (praying for evil).

"Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]" (اللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ).

This is an indication that those who spread corruption on earth to gain more income, sever Divine bonds, and break Allah's covenant to acquire more profit and benefit from material life, are heedless of the reality that sustenance—its abundance or scarcity—is in the hand of Allah.

Furthermore, this sentence can be an answer to a question not explicitly stated in the verse, which is: After describing the two groups—the supporters of truth and falsehood—in the previous verses, the question arises: How does Allah allow all of them to enjoy His blessings and sustenance?!

In response to this question, the verse says: Sustenance and its increase or decrease are in Allah's hand, and in any case, it is a fleeting enjoyment. What must be accounted for is the other abode and eternal felicity.

However, the important point is that "Divine Will" regarding sustenance does not mean that Allah gives abundant provision to someone or restricts it without account or without the use of means. Rather, His Will is established such that it be sought through the means of this world, for "Allah refuses to let affairs proceed except through their means" (أبى الله أن يجري الأمور إلا بأسبابها)! Then it adds that "the covenant-breakers and mischief-makers on earth are only pleased and happy with the life of this world, while the life of this world, compared to the Hereafter, is but a brief passing enjoyment" (وَ فَرِحُوا بِالْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ مَا الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتاعٌ).

The mention of "enjoyment" (mata') in the indefinite form is to express its insignificance, just as we say in Persian that a certain matter is "but a [mere] commodity," meaning a worthless one!

Key Points

1- Who is a Mischief-Maker on Earth (Mufsid fil-Ard)?

Corruption (fasad), which is the opposite of righteousness (salah), refers to any kind of destruction and ruin. According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat: "Fasad is the departure of a thing from moderation, whether slightly or greatly; its opposite is salah. It is used regarding the soul, the body, and external things that deviate from uprightness."

Therefore, all disorders created in affairs, and all excesses and deficiencies in individual and social issues, are instances of corruption. In the Holy Quran, "corruption" and "righteousness" are frequently placed in opposition to each other. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 152, we read: الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا يُصْلِحُونَ: "Those who spread corruption in the land and do not reform."

In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 220, He says: وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْمُفْسِدَ مِنَ الْمُصْلِحِ: "And Allah knows the corrupter from the reformer."

And in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 142, we read: وَ أَصْلِحْ وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ: "Act righteously and do not follow the way of the corrupters."

In some instances, faith and righteous deeds are placed in opposition to corruption: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ: "Shall We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like corrupters in the land?" (Surah Sad, verse 27).

On the other hand, in many verses of the Quran, "corruption" is mentioned alongside the phrase "in the land" (fil-ard), which shows a focus on the social aspects of the issue. This expression appears in over twenty places in the Quran.

Thirdly, "corruption and causing corruption" (fasad and ifsad) are mentioned in various verses of the Holy Quran alongside other sins that are perhaps mostly examples of it. Some of these sins are exceptionally great, while others are smaller. For example:

Sometimes it is mentioned alongside waging war against Allah and the Prophet, such as: إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ وَ يَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً (Al-Ma'idah, 33).

Sometimes it is alongside the destruction of crops and progeny (humans and agriculture): وَ إِذا تَوَلَّى سَعى‌ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُفْسِدَ فِيها وَ يُهْلِكَ الْحَرْثَ وَ النَّسْلَ‌ (Al-Baqarah, 205).

And sometimes alongside severing the ties Allah has commanded to be joined: الَّذِينَ يَنْقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ مِيثاقِهِ وَ يَقْطَعُونَ ما أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَنْ يُوصَلَ وَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌ (Al-Baqarah, 27).

And sometimes it is placed alongside seeking superiority and rebellion: تِلْكَ الدَّارُ الْآخِرَةُ نَجْعَلُها لِلَّذِينَ لا يُرِيدُونَ عُلُوًّا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا فَساداً: "That is the home of the Hereafter; We assign it to those who do not desire exaltedness upon the earth or corruption" (Al-Qasas, 83).

Sometimes it considers Pharaoh a mischief-maker, and at the time of his repentance while drowning in the Nile, it says: آلْآنَ وَ قَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ: "Now? And you had disobeyed before and were of the corrupters" (Yunus, 91).

Also, this term (corruption in the land) is sometimes used regarding theft, as we read in the story of Joseph's brothers, who said after being accused of theft: تَاللَّهِ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمْ ما جِئْنا لِنُفْسِدَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ ما كُنَّا سارِقِينَ: "By Allah, you certainly know that we did not come to cause corruption in the land, and we have not been thieves" (Yusuf, 73).

And sometimes it is mentioned alongside short-changing (cheating in weights), as we read in the story of Shu'ayb عليه السلام: وَ لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ: "And do not deprive people of their due and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption" (Hud, 85).

Finally, it sometimes means the disruption of the order of the universe and the world of creation: لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتا: "Had there been within them [the heavens and earth] gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined" (Al-Anbiya, 22).

From the sum of these verses appearing in various surahs of the Quran, it is clearly understood that corruption in general, or "corruption in the land," has a very broad meaning. It encompasses the greatest crimes, such as those of Pharaoh and other tyrants, as well as lesser acts, and even sins like short-changing and fraud in transactions. Given the broad concept of corruption—which includes any departure from moderation—this scope is fully understandable.

Considering that punishments must correspond to the magnitude of the crime, it becomes clear that each group of these "mischief-makers" must be punished in a specific way, and the punishment for all is not uniform.

Even in verse 33 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, where "mischief-maker in the land" is mentioned alongside "one who wars against Allah and the Prophet," we see four types of punishments. The Sharia judge must choose the punishment in each case that is proportionate to the extent of the crime (execution, crucifixion, amputation of hands and feet, and exile). Our jurists have detailed the conditions and limits of these punishments in books of jurisprudence under the discussion of the "Muharib" and "Mufsid fil-Ard." [^1] Furthermore, to uproot such corruptions, appropriate means must be employed in each case. Sometimes the first stage of "enjoining good and forbidding evil"—namely advice, counsel, and reminders—is sufficient; but a time also comes when one must resort to the highest degree of severity, which is war.
