Additionally, the expression "corruption in the land" guides us to a reality in human social life: social vices are usually not localized and cannot be confined to one region; rather, they spread across society and the earth, infecting one group after another.

This point is also clearly derived from the Quranic verses: one of the great goals of the mission of the Prophets was to end every kind of corruption on earth (in its broad sense). As we read from the words of Shu'ayb, the great Prophet of Allah: إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ: He said in the face of the corruption of that rebellious people, "I only desire reform as much as I am able" (Hud, 88).

2- Sustenance is in Allah's Hand, But! ...

It is not only the above verses that say the increase and decrease of sustenance is in Allah's hand; this same meaning is clearly understood from various other verses of the Quran—that Allah expands provision for whom He wills and restricts it for whom He wills. However, this statement does not mean what some ignorant people have imagined: that one should abandon activity and sit in a corner until Allah brings what is predestined. Such individuals, whose negative thinking provides a good excuse for those who consider religion an "opiate," are heedless of two fundamental points:

First, the Divine Will and Intention referred to in these verses is not an arbitrary or unaccounted matter. Rather, as we have said before, Allah's Will is not separate from His Wisdom and is always based on merit and worthiness.

Second: This issue does not mean the negation of the world of causes. For the world of causes—the world of creation—is also the Will of Allah and His creative Will, and it is never separate from His legislative Will.

In clearer terms, Allah's Will regarding the expansion or restriction of sustenance is conditional upon the circumstances governing human life. Efforts, struggles, sincerity, and sacrifices—and conversely, lethargy, laziness, stinginess, and impurity of intentions—play a decisive role in it. For this reason, the Holy Quran repeatedly considers man to be hostage to his own effort, struggle, and activity, and deems his share of life to be in proportion to his effort and strive.

Therefore, in the book Wasa'il al-Shi'a, in the "Book of Trade and Business," a chapter is titled regarding the effort and struggle to obtain sustenance, and several hadiths are mentioned in this regard. Other chapters have also been established in condemnation of "idleness," "excessive sleep," and "laziness" in the path of acquiring life's necessities.

Among them, in a hadith narrated from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام in these chapters, we read that he said: "When things were paired at the beginning, laziness and incapacity paired together, and from them, a child named 'poverty' was born!" [^1]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Do not be lazy in seeking your livelihood, for our fathers and ancestors used to run for it and seek it!" [^2]

[^1] and [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, p. 38.

In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "I indeed detest a man who is lazy regarding his worldly affairs; and whoever is lazy regarding his worldly affairs is even lazier regarding the affairs of his Hereafter." [^1]

And it is also narrated from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام that he said: "Indeed, Allah Almighty detests the servant who sleeps excessively; indeed, Allah detests the idle person." [^2]

[^1] and [^2]: Wasa'il, Vol. 12, p. 37.

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنَابَ (٢٧)
The faithless say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides to Himself those who turn penitently [to Him] (27)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ (٢٨)
—those who have faith and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah.’ Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance! (28)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ طُوبَىٰ لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَـَٔابٍ (٢٩)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds—happy are they and good is their [ultimate] destination. (29)

Commentary

Remembrance of Allah is the Source of Peace for Hearts

Since many discussions in this Surah revolve around Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the Mission of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), the first verse under discussion once again addresses the matter of the invitation of the Prophet of Islam and states one of the objections of the stubborn deniers:

"And those who disbelieve say, 'Why has a sign (miracle) not been sent down to him from his Lord?'" (وَ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ‌).

The word "يقول" (they say) is in the present continuous tense, indicating that they repeated this objection many times. Despite having repeatedly witnessed miracles from the Prophet (and while it is the duty of every Prophet to provide evidence of his truthfulness through miracles), they continued to make excuses, ignoring past miracles and demanding new ones of their own choosing.

In other words, they and all stubborn deniers constantly seek "suggested miracles" (mu'jizat iqtirahi). They expect the Prophet to sit in a corner like a "performer of the extraordinary," and as each of them enters, they propose whatever miracle they fancy, expecting him to present it immediately—and even then, they might still choose not to believe!

However, we know that the primary duty of Prophets is preaching, teaching, warning, and admonishing. Extraordinary acts are exceptional matters performed only to the extent of necessity and by the command of Allah (not at the whim of the Prophet). Yet, we repeatedly read in the verses of the Quran that groups of antagonists, heedless of this reality, constantly harassed the Prophets with such requests.

The Quran says in response to them: "Say [O Prophet], 'Indeed, Allah leaves astray whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back [to Him]'" (قُلْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ‌).

This is an indication that your deficiency does not stem from a lack of miracles, for the Prophet has presented sufficient miracles. Your deficiency lies within your own being: the stubbornness, prejudices, ignorance, and sins that block the path of success and prevent your faith.

Therefore, return to Allah and turn to Him in repentance (inabah). Remove the veils of ignorance, pride, and selfishness from before the eyes of your intellect and thought so that you may clearly see the beauty of the Truth, for: The beauty of the Beloved has no veil or curtain, But settle the dust of the road so that you may behold!

In the next verse, it provides a very beautiful explanation of "those who turn back" (man anab): "Those who have believed and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ تَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ‌). And then, as a general rule and an eternal, all-encompassing principle, He declares: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" (أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ‌).

In the final verse under discussion, by mentioning the fate and end of the believers, He completes the theme of the previous verses: "Those who have believed and done righteous deeds—Tuba (blessedness/the best life) is for them and a good return" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ طُوبى‌ لَهُمْ وَ حُسْنُ مَآبٍ‌).

Many great commentators consider the word "Tuba" to be the feminine form of "Atyab," meaning better and purer, or the best and purest. Given that its object is omitted, the concept of this word is broad and unlimited in every respect. Consequently, by mentioning the phrase "طُوبى‌ لَهُمْ‌," all goodness and purity are envisioned for them—the best of everything: the best life, the best blessings, the best peace, the best friends, and the best of the Lord's special favors. All of these are contingent upon faith and righteous deeds, and they are a reward for those who are firm in belief and pure, active, upright, and helpful in action.

Therefore, the various interpretations mentioned by a group of commentators—to the extent that ten meanings are listed in Majma' al-Bayan—are all, in fact, different instances of this broad meaning.

In numerous narrations, we also read that "Tuba" is a tree whose root is in the house of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) or Ali (peace be upon him) in Paradise, and its branches extend everywhere, over all believers and above all their houses. This may actually be a manifestation of their position of leadership and guidance, and the unbreakable bonds between these great leaders and their followers, the fruit of which is all those diverse and varied blessings.

(And if we see "Tuba" mentioned in the feminine form rather than the masculine "Atyab," it is because it serves as an adjective for "life" (hayat) or "blessing" (ni'mah), both of which are feminine in Arabic).

Key Points

1- How Does the Heart Find Rest with the Remembrance of Allah?

Anxiety and worry have always been among the greatest afflictions of human life, and their effects are clearly felt in individual and social life.

Peace has always been one of humanity's most important lost treasures, and man knocks on every door to find it. If we were to compile the efforts and struggles of humans throughout history to find peace through "correct" and "false" means, it would form a very thick book.

Some scientists say: during the outbreak of certain contagious diseases like "cholera," out of every ten people who apparently die, the majority die due to worry and fear, and only a minority die truly because of contracting the disease!

In general, "peace" and "anxiety" play a very important role in the "health" and "illness" of the individual and society, and the happiness or misery of humans. It is not something that can be easily overlooked. For this reason, many books have been written solely on the subject of worry, how to combat it, and how to attain peace.

Human history is full of tragic scenes where man has grasped at anything and stepped into every valley, even succumbing to various addictions, to achieve peace.

But the Quran, with one short and profound sentence, shows the most reliable and nearest path, saying: "Know that the remembrance of Allah brings peace to hearts!" To clarify this Quranic truth, consider the following explanation:

Factors of Worry and Distress

  1. Sometimes anxiety and worry are due to a dark and ambiguous future that looms before human thought: the possibility of the loss of blessings, falling into the clutches of an enemy, weakness, illness, disability, helplessness, and need. All these cause man suffering. However, faith in Allah the Almighty, the All-Merciful and Compassionate—the God who always takes responsibility for His servants—can eliminate such worries and give him peace, knowing that he is not helpless against future events; he has a God who is Powerful, Capable, and Kind.

  2. Sometimes the dark past of life occupies human thought and constantly worries him: worry over sins committed, shortcomings, and slips. However, turning to the fact that Allah is the All-Forgiver, the Accepter of Repentance, the Merciful, and the Pardoning gives him peace. it tells him: take your excuse for your faults to His threshold, apologize for the past, and rise to compensate, for He is Forgiving and compensation is possible.

  3. Human weakness and inability against natural factors, and sometimes against a multitude of internal and external enemies, make him worry: "What shall I do against all these powerful enemies in the field of Jihad?" or "What can I achieve in other struggles?" But when he remembers Allah and relies on His power and mercy—a power that is the highest of powers and against which nothing can resist—his heart finds rest. He says to himself: "Yes, I am not alone; under the shadow of Allah, I have infinite power!" The heroism of the warriors in the way of Allah in battles, past and present, and their amazing and stunning bravery, even when they were alone, expresses the peace found under the shadow of faith. When we see with our own eyes and hear with our ears that a brave officer, after a stunning battle, has completely lost his sight and lies on a hospital bed with a wounded body, yet speaks with such peace of mind and assurance as if not even a scratch has touched his body, we realize the miracle of peace under the shadow of the remembrance of Allah. [^1]

  4. Sometimes the root of man's agonizing worries is the feeling of the futility of life and its lack of purpose. But he who believes in Allah and has accepted the evolutionary path of life as a great goal, and sees all programs and events of life in this line, neither feels futility in life nor is he wandering and anxious like aimless and hesitant individuals.

[^1]: This event, which occurred during the recent Iran-Iraq war—a war imposed on our country by enemies—was not just a single case; there were hundreds and thousands of examples reflecting the bravery and struggles of the warriors of Badr and other Islamic wars.

  1. Another factor of worry is that man sometimes endures great hardship to reach a goal, but sees no one to value his hardship or show appreciation and gratitude. This ingratitude causes him severe suffering and plunges him into a state of anxiety. But when he feels that someone is aware of all his efforts and struggles, values them all, and rewards them all, what room is there for worry and restlessness?

  2. Suspicion, illusions, and vain imaginations are other factors of worry from which many people suffer in their lives. But how can it be denied that turning to Allah and His infinite grace, and the command to have good expectations (husn al-zann) which is the duty of every person of faith, eliminates this painful state and replaces it with peace and assurance?

  3. Worldliness and being infatuated with the glitz and glamour of material life have been and are among the greatest factors of human anxiety. To the extent that sometimes the inability to obtain a specific color of clothing, shoes, or a hat, or one of thousands of other life amenities, keeps the thoughts of worldlings restless and disturbed for hours, days, or weeks. But faith in Allah and attention to the freedom of the believer—which is always accompanied by "constructive asceticism and piety" and lack of captivity in the clutches of the glitz of material life—ends all these anxieties. When the human soul expands in an "Ali-like" manner such that he says: "Your world, to me, is more worthless than a leaf in the mouth of a locust that is chewing it," [^1] how is it possible that not attaining a material tool or losing it could disturb the peace of the human soul and create a storm of worry in his heart and mind?

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 224.

  1. Another important factor for worry is the fear and dread of death, which has always tormented the human soul. Since the possibility of death exists not only in old age but at other ages, especially during illnesses, wars, and insecurity, this worry can be universal. But if, from a worldview perspective, we consider death to mean annihilation, non-existence, and the end of everything (as the materialists of the world imagine), this anxiety and worry are completely justified. One should fear such a death, which is the end point of all human hopes, successes, and desires. However, whenever, under the shadow of faith in Allah, we consider death a gateway to a wider and loftier life, and regard passing through the gateway of death like passing through the corridor of a prison to reach an open space, then this worry becomes meaningless. Rather, such a death—whenever it is in the path of fulfilling a duty—is lovable and desirable. Of course, the factors of worry are not limited to these; many other factors can be counted. But it must be accepted that most worries return to one of the above factors. And when we see that these factors melt, fade, and vanish before faith in Allah, we will testify that the remembrance of Allah is the source of peace for hearts: "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" (أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ‌). [^1]

2- Is Peace Compatible with the Fear of Allah?

Some commentators have raised an objection here, the summary of which is:

[^1]: For further explanation, please refer to the book "The Way to Overcome Worries and Despairs."

On one hand, we read in the above verse that the remembrance of Allah is the source of peace for hearts, and on the other hand, in verse 2 of Surah Al-Anfal, we read: "The believers are only those who, when Allah is mentioned, their hearts tremble" (إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ‌). Are these two not contradictory?

The answer to the question is that the intended "peace" is peace in the face of material factors that worry most people, clear examples of which were mentioned above. But certainly, people of faith cannot be without concern regarding their responsibilities. In other words, what does not exist in them are destructive worries, which constitute the majority of anxieties. However, constructive worry, which drives man to fulfill his duty toward Allah and creation and toward positive life activities, exists in them and must exist. This is also the meaning of the fear of Allah. [^1]

3- What is the Remembrance of Allah and How is it Performed?

"Dhikr" (Remembrance), as Raghib stated in Al-Mufradat, "sometimes comes with the meaning of preserving information and knowledge, with the difference that the word 'hifz' (preservation) is applied to its beginning and 'dhikr' to its continuation. Sometimes it means recalling something by tongue or by heart. Therefore, they have said dhikr is of two types: 'heartfelt dhikr' and 'verbal dhikr,' and each of them is of two types: either after forgetting or without forgetting."

In any case, the meaning of the remembrance of Allah in the above verse, which is the source of peace for hearts, is not merely to utter His name on the tongue and repeatedly say Tasbih, Tahlil, and Takbir. Rather, it means to turn toward Him with the whole heart, toward His greatness, His knowledge and awareness, and His being present and watching. This attention should become the origin of movement and activity in one's being toward Jihad, effort, and goodness, and create a solid barrier between him and sin. This is the reality of "Dhikr," for which all those effects and blessings have been stated in Islamic narrations.

In a Hadith, we read that among the testaments the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) gave to Ali (peace be upon him) was: "O Ali, there are three things that this Ummah cannot endure (and not everyone is capable of them): sharing wealth with a brother in faith, giving people their rights from oneself, and the remembrance of Allah in every state. And it is not [just] 'Subhan Allah,' 'Alhamdulillah,' 'La ilaha illa Allah,' and 'Allahu Akbar,' but rather the remembrance of Allah is that when a person encounters what is forbidden to him, he fears Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and leaves it." [^1]

In another Hadith, we read that Ali (peace be upon him) said: "Dhikr is of two types: remembering Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, at the time of calamity (and practicing patience and steadfastness), and better than that is remembering Allah regarding what He has forbidden to you, so that it acts as a barrier." [^2]

It is for this reason that in some narrations, the remembrance of Allah is counted as a shield and a means of defense. In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read that one day the Prophet turned to his companions and said: "Take up your shields." They said, "O Messenger of Allah, is it against an enemy that has overshadowed us?" He said, "No, but against the Fire (of Hell). Say: Subhan Allah, wal-Hamdulillah, wa la ilaha illa Allah, wallahu Akbar (Glorify Allah's purity, thank Him for His blessings, choose no deity besides Him, and consider Him greater than everything)." [^1]

And if we see in some Hadiths that the Prophet is introduced as "Dhikr Allah" (the Remembrance of Allah), it is also because he reminds people of Allah and educates them. It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the interpretation of "Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured" that he said: "Through Muhammad, hearts find peace, and he is the Remembrance of Allah and His Veil!"

[^1] and [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 484. [^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 484.

Ayahs

كَذَٰلِكَ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ فِىٓ أُمَّةٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهَآ أُمَمٌ لِّتَتْلُوَا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ قُلْ هُوَ رَبِّى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ مَتَابِ (٣٠)
Thus have We sent you to a nation before which many nations have passed away, that you may recite to them what We have revealed to you. Yet they defy the All-beneficent. Say, ‘He is my Lord; there is no god except Him; in Him I have put my trust, and to Him will be my return.’ (30)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْءَانًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ ٱلْجِبَالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۗ بَل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا ۗ أَفَلَمْ يَا۟يْـَٔسِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَى ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ تُصِيبُهُم بِمَا صَنَعُوا۟ قَارِعَةٌ أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيبًا مِّن دَارِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ وَعْدُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (٣١)
If only it were a Quran whereby the mountains could be moved, or the earth could be toured, or the dead could be spoken to.... Indeed, all dispensation belongs to Allah. Have not the faithful yet realised that had Allah wished He would have guided mankind all together? The faithless will continue to be visited by catastrophes because of their doings—or they will land near their habitations—until Allah’s promise comes to pass. Indeed Allah does not break His promise. (31)
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ (٣٢)
Apostles were certainly derided before you. But then I gave respite to those who were faithless, then I seized them; so how was My retribution? (32)

Occasion of Revelation

Commentators have stated that the first verse was revealed during the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in the sixth year of the Hijra. At the time when they intended to write the peace treaty, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "Write: In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful (بسم اللَّه الرحمن الرحيم)..." Suhayl ibn Amr and the other polytheists said: "We do not know 'the Most Gracious' (al-Rahman)! We only know one 'Rahman', and he is in Yamama" (referring to Musaylimah the Liar who claimed prophethood). "Rather, you must write: 'In Your name, O Allah' (باسمك اللهم), just as it was written during the Age of Ignorance." Then the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "Write: This is the peace treaty which Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah صلي الله عليه وآله..." The polytheists of Quraish said: "If you were the Messenger of Allah and we fought you and barred you from the House of Allah, we would have been great oppressors (the very dispute is over your messengership); rather, write: This is the peace treaty of Muhammad ibn Abdillah!..."

At this moment, the companions of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله became enraged and said: "Permit us to fight them." The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله said: "No, write it as they desire." At this point, the above verse was revealed, severely rebuking them for their stubbornness, excuse-making, and opposition to the name "al-Rahman," which is one of the definitive attributes of Allah.

This occasion of revelation is correct only if we consider this Surah to be "Madani" (revealed in Medina) so that it aligns with the story of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah; however, if we consider it "Makki" (revealed in Mecca)—as is the popular view—this discussion does not apply.

Unless we consider the occasion of revelation for this verse to be a response to the words of the polytheists mentioned in Surah Al-Furqan, where, in response to the Prophet's صلي الله عليه وآله invitation to prostrate to al-Rahman, they said: "We do not know al-Rahman" (اسْجُدُوا لِلرَّحْمنِ قالُوا وَ مَا الرَّحْمنُ‌—Surah Al-Furqan, verse 60). In any case, the above verse, regardless of the occasion of revelation, has a clear meaning which you will read in its commentary.

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the second verse, some great commentators have said it was revealed in response to a group of polytheists from Mecca who were sitting behind the Kaaba and sent for the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله came to them (hoping for their guidance). They said: "If you wish for us to follow you, then move these mountains of Mecca back by means of your Quran so that this narrow and limited land of ours may expand! And split the earth and bring forth springs and rivers here so that we may plant trees and practice agriculture! You, in your own estimation, are not less than David, for whom Allah had subjected the mountains to echo his voice in glorifying God. Or subject the wind to us so that we may ride upon it to Sham (Syria) to solve our problems, procure our needs, and return on the same day! Just as it was subjected to Solomon; you, in your own estimation, are not less than Solomon. Also, resurrect your ancestor 'Qusayy' (the ancestor of the Quraish tribe) or anyone else from our dead so that we may ask him whether what you say is truth or falsehood, for Jesus عليه السلام used to raise the dead and you are not less than Jesus!" At this moment, the second verse under discussion was revealed, reminding them that all they say is out of stubbornness and not for the sake of believing; otherwise, sufficient miracles have already been presented for one to believe.

Commentary

No Matter What You Do, These Stubborn People Will Not Believe!

Once again, in these verses, we return to the discussion of prophethood. The above verses recount another part of the polytheists' dialogues and the responses to them regarding prophethood:

First, it says: "Thus have We sent you to a nation before whom other nations have passed away" (كَذلِكَ أَرْسَلْناكَ فِي أُمَّةٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِها أُمَمٌ‌).

"And the purpose was that you might recite to them what We have revealed to you" (لِتَتْلُوَا عَلَيْهِمُ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ‌).

"While they disbelieve in 'al-Rahman'" (in the God whose mercy encompasses everyone and whose widespread and general grace includes the disbeliever, the believer, the fire-worshipper, and the Christian) (وَ هُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِالرَّحْمنِ‌).

"Say: If you do not know, al-Rahman—that God whose grace and mercy are universal—is my Lord" (قُلْ هُوَ رَبِّي‌).

"There is no deity except Him; in Him I have put my trust, and to Him is my return" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَ إِلَيْهِ مَتابِ‌).

Then, in response to these excuse-seekers who find fault with everything, He says: "And even if there were a Quran by which mountains were moved, or the earth were torn asunder, or the dead were made to speak, they would still not believe!" (وَ لَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ الْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ الْمَوْتى‌).

"But to Allah belongs the command entirely" and He performs as much as He deems necessary (بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً).

But you are not seekers of truth; if you were, the amount of miraculous signs that have proceeded from this Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله would have been entirely sufficient for believing. All of this is mere excuse-making.

Then He adds: "Have those who believe not known that if Allah willed, He could have guided all of humanity [by compulsion]?" (أَ فَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللَّهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً). [1] This is an indication that Allah is able, through internal or external compulsion, to force even the stubborn deniers to accept faith, for He is powerful over all things, and no task is difficult for His power. However, He will never do such a thing, because such a forced faith is worthless and lacks the spirituality and perfection that a human being requires.

Next, He adds that "at the same time, the disbelievers are constantly struck by crushing calamities because of their deeds," which descend upon them in the form of various afflictions as well as the crushing wars of the mujahideen of Islam (وَ لا يَزالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا تُصِيبُهُمْ بِما صَنَعُوا قارِعَةٌ).

"And if these calamities do not descend upon their very homes, they land near their homes" (أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيباً مِنْ دارِهِمْ‌).

So that they might take heed, be shaken, and return to Allah.

"And these warnings will continue until the final promise of Allah comes to pass" (حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ وَعْدُ اللَّهِ‌).

[1]: "أَ فَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ" is from the root "ya's" meaning despair, but many commentators have said that here it means "to know" (ilm). However, according to "Kisa'i" (as quoted by Fakhr al-Razi), it has never been seen that "ya'istu" means "alimtu" (I knew). From the words of Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it appears that "ya's" here carries its well-known meaning, but the prerequisite of any despair is the knowledge that the matter will not be realized. Therefore, the establishment of their despair necessitates their knowledge. However, the result of Raghib's statement would be that "ya's" here does not mean knowledge of existence, but rather knowledge of non-existence, which does not align with the context of the verse. Therefore, the truth is what the majority of commentators have said, and they have brought evidence from Arabic speech for it; Fakhr al-Razi has cited examples of such evidence in his Tafsir (observe closely).

This final command may refer to death, or the Day of Resurrection, or as some have said, the "Conquest of Mecca" which shattered the enemy's final power.

In any case, Allah's promise is certain, "and Allah never fails in His promise" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ).

The last verse under discussion addresses the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, saying: You are not the only one who has been subjected to mockery and ridicule through various demands and the proposal of arbitrary miracles by this group of disbelievers; this has a long history in the annals of the Prophets: "And certainly, messengers before you were mocked" (وَ لَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ‌).

But We did not punish these disbelievers immediately, "rather, I gave respite to those who disbelieved" (فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا).

Perhaps they would wake up and return to the path of truth, or at least so that the proof against them would be completed. For if they are evildoers and sinners, Allah's kindness, grace, generosity, and wisdom have not vanished! But in any case, this respite and delay do not mean that their punishment and penalty are forgotten; therefore, "then I seized them, and how [terrible] was My punishment!" This fate awaits your stubborn people as well (ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ عِقابِ‌).

Key Points

1- Why is the word "al-Rahman" emphasized?

The above verses and the occasion of revelation mentioned regarding them show that the disbelievers of Quraish did not like the description of Allah as "al-Rahman," and since such a thing was not customary among them, they took it as a subject of mockery. Meanwhile, the above verses emphasize and insist upon it, because a specific grace is hidden in this word. We know that the attribute of "Rahmaniyyah" (General Mercy) of Allah refers to His universal grace that encompasses friend and foe alike, and both the believer and the disbeliever are included in it, in contrast to the attribute of "Rahimiyyah" (Specific Mercy) which refers to the specific divine mercy regarding righteous servants and believers.

That is to say: How can you not believe in a God who is the source of grace and generosity and who includes even His enemies in His grace and mercy? This is the height of your ignorance.

2- Why did the Prophet not submit to the demand for miracles?

Here again, we encounter the words of those who imagine that the Prophet of Islam had no miracle other than the Quran, and they seek help from verses similar to the ones above, because the appearance of these verses says that the Prophet rejected the requesters in the face of demands for various miracles such as moving the mountains of Mecca, splitting the land there, revealing rivers, and resurrecting the dead and speaking with them.

But we have said many times that a miracle is only for showing the face of truth to those who are seekers of truth, not so that the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله becomes a miracle-worker where he performs whatever action anyone proposes, and even then they are not ready to accept it.

This type of request for miracles (arbitrary miracles) was only made by stubborn and light-headed individuals who were not ready to accept any truth.

Coincidentally, the signs of this matter are clearly visible in the above verses, for in the last verse under discussion, we saw that there was mention of the mockery of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله; meaning they did not want to see the face of truth, but rather they wanted to mock the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله with these proposals!

Furthermore, from the occasions of revelation we read at the beginning of these verses, it is understood that they had proposed to the Prophet to resurrect one of their ancestors so they could ask him whether he was on the truth or falsehood. While if the Prophet were to perform such a miracle (resurrecting the dead), there would no longer be any place for them to ask whether the Prophet is on the truth or falsehood! This shows that they were prejudiced, stubborn, and antagonistic individuals whose goal was not searching for the truth; they always made strange and wondrous proposals and yet, in the end, they would not believe (God willing, we will explain this further under verse 90 of Surah Al-Isra).

3- What is "al-Qari'ah"?

"Qari'ah" is from the root "qar'" meaning to strike. Therefore, "Qari'ah" means "the striker," and here it refers to matters that strike a person and warn them, and if they are ready to wake up, they wake up.

In reality, "Qari'ah" has a broad meaning that includes any kind of personal or collective calamity, and painful problems and events.

Therefore, some commentators have interpreted it to mean wars, droughts, killings, and being taken captive, while others have taken it to refer only to the wars that occurred in the early days of Islam under the title of "Sariyyah"—which were wars in which the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله did not personally participate but assigned the mission to his companions. However, it is certain that "Qari'ah" is not exclusive to any of these matters and encompasses them all.

It is interesting that in the above verses we read that these crushing events either reach them personally or land near their homes; meaning that even if they themselves are not afflicted by these warning events, they occur in their neighborhood or vicinity. Is this not enough to wake them up?

Ayahs

أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قَآئِمٌ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۗ وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ ۚ أَمْ تُنَبِّـُٔونَهُۥ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَم بِظَـٰهِرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۗ بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مَكْرُهُمْ وَصُدُّوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٣)
Is He who sustains every soul in spite of what it earns [comparable to the idols]? Yet they ascribe partners to Allah! Say, ‘Name them!’ Will you inform Him concerning something He does not know about on the earth, or concerning [what are] mere words? Indeed, to the faithless, their scheming is presented as decorous, and they have been barred from the [right] way; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. (33)
لَّهُمْ عَذَابٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَقُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ (٣٤)
There is a punishment for them in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter will surely be harder, and they have no defender against Allah. (34)

Commentary

How Can You Associate Partners with Allah?!

In these verses, the discussion returns once again to the topic of Monotheism (Tawhid) and Polytheism (Shirk), addressing the people with this clear reasoning: "Is He who is the Guardian of everything across the expanse of existence, who has placed everything under the cover of His governance, and is aware and informed of the actions of everyone, like one who possesses none of these attributes?!" (أَ فَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى‌ كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ‌) [^1]. In fact, the aforementioned phrase clearly states that His encompassment is such that it is as if Allah is standing over everyone, seeing what they do, knowing it, holding them to account, rewarding and punishing, and managing and disposing of all affairs. Therefore, the word "Qa'im" (Standing/Guardian) has a broad meaning that includes all these matters, although some commentators have limited it to only one dimension.

Then, as a completion of the previous discussion and a preamble for the upcoming one, He says:

"They have attributed partners to Allah" (وَ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ).

Immediately, He responds to them through several avenues.

First, He says: "Name them!" (قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ‌).

The purpose of "naming them" is either to imply that they are even more worthless than having a name or identity—meaning, how can you place a group of nameless, identityless, and worthless beings in the same rank as the Almighty and All-Powerful Lord?

Or, the purpose is to say: "Describe their attributes so we may see if they are worthy of worship." Regarding Allah, we say He is the Creator, the Provider, the Giver of Life, the All-Knowing, the All-Powerful, and the Great. Can you apply these attributes to the idols? Or conversely, if we want to name them, we must say: idols made of stone and wood, motionless,

[^1]: In reality, the above sentence contains a subject (mubtada) whose predicate (khabar) is omitted. The implied meaning is: "Is He who possesses this attribute like one who lacks it?" (أ فمن هو قائم على كل نفس بما كسبت كمن ليس كذلك).

lacking intellect and consciousness, dependent upon their worshippers, and in short, lacking everything! How can these two be considered equal? Is this not shameful?

Or, the purpose is to say: "Enumerate their deeds." Have they ever caused harm to anyone or provided any benefit? Have they solved a problem or untied a knot in anyone's affairs? Despite this, what intellect permits placing them in the same rank as the God from whom all blessings, favors, benefits, harms, rewards, and punishments originate?

Of course, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined in the phrase "Name them" (Sammuhum).

Second: How can such a partner exist when Allah—who according to your fancy is their partner—has no knowledge of their existence, even though His knowledge encompasses the entire universe? "Or do you inform Him of something He does not know on the earth?" (أَمْ تُنَبِّئُونَهُ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌).

This expression is, in fact, the best way to demolish the opponent's hollow argument. It is like someone telling you, "Last night, so-and-so was a guest in your house," and you reply, "You are informing me of a guest of whom I am unaware!" That is to say, is it possible for someone to be my guest while I am unaware and you are informed of it?

Third: You yourselves do not actually believe in such a thing in your hearts; "You merely content yourselves with empty outward speech in which no correct concept is hidden" (أَمْ بِظاهِرٍ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ‌).

For this reason, when these polytheists find themselves in the severe straits of life, they turn to "Allah," because they know in their hearts that idols can do nothing. This is just as Allah has described their state when they board a ship and a fierce storm surrounds them, and they turn only to God, in verse 65 of Surah Al-Ankabut.

Fourth: These polytheists lack correct and sound understanding. Because they follow whims, desires, and blind imitation, they are incapable of rational and correct judgment. For this reason, they have been drawn into this deviation and misguidance: "Their plotting and schemes against the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, and their lies, slanders, and deceptions have been made fair-seeming to them (due to their inner impurity)," to the point that they have considered these worthless and nameless beings as partners to God (بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مَكْرُهُمْ وَ صُدُّوا عَنِ السَّبِيلِ‌).

"And whomever Allah leaves to stray, for him there will be no guide" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ).

We have said many times that this "straying" does not mean compulsion, nor is it a matter of whim or without reason. Rather, Divine misguidance means the reaction to the person's own incorrect and wrong actions which lead them into wanderings. Since Allah has placed this property in such actions, it is attributed to Him.

In the final verse under discussion, He refers to their painful punishments in this world and the hereafter—which naturally include defeat, failure, misery, disgrace, and more—saying: "For them is a punishment in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe and harder" (لَهُمْ عَذابٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ لَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَشَقُ‌). This is because it is permanent, eternal, physical, spiritual, and coupled with all kinds of distress.

And if they imagine they have a way of escape or a means of defense against it, they are gravely mistaken, for "They have no defender against Allah" (وَ ما لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ واقٍ‌).

Ayah

۞ مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ أُكُلُهَا دَآئِمٌ وَظِلُّهَا ۚ تِلْكَ عُقْبَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ ۖ وَّعُقْبَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ٱلنَّارُ (٣٥)
A description of the paradise promised to the Godwary: streams run in it, its fruits and shade are everlasting. Such is the requital of those who are Godwary; and the requital of the faithless is the Fire. (35)

Commentary

Considering the alternation of verses in this Surah regarding the explanation of Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and other Islamic teachings, this verse once again speaks of the Resurrection, specifically regarding the blessings of Paradise and the punishments of Hell.

First, it says: "The description of the Paradise promised to the righteous is that of gardens beneath which rivers flow" (مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ) [^1]. The use of the word "Mathal" (description/likeness) perhaps points to the fact that the gardens and other blessings of the next world cannot be fully described by any statement for the inhabitants of this limited world—which is incredibly small compared to the world after death. Only a likeness or a shadow of it can be depicted for the people of this world, just as if a fetus in the womb possessed intellect and consciousness, it would be impossible to explain the blessings of this world to it except through imperfect and faint examples!

[^1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding the syntax of this sentence. Some consider "Mathal" to be the subject (mubtada) and "tajri" to be its predicate (khabar). Others consider "Mathal" to be the subject with its predicate omitted, implying: "Among the things related to you is the description of Paradise."

The second description of the gardens of Paradise is that "its fruit is eternal and everlasting" (أُكُلُها دائِمٌ).

It is not like the fruits of this world which are seasonal, each appearing in a specific time; rather, sometimes due to pests or calamities, they may not exist at all in a given year. However, the fruits of Paradise have no pests, nor are they seasonal or occasional; rather, like the faith of the true believers, they are eternal and steadfast.

And likewise, "its shade is also everlasting" (وَ ظِلُّها).

It is not like the shadows of the trees in this world, where there might be a thick shade across the garden in the early morning when the sun shines from one side, but at noon when the sun shines vertically, the shadows diminish; or they exist in spring and summer when trees are full of leaves and vanish in autumn and winter when trees become bare (of course, small examples of evergreen trees that always provide fruit and flowers are seen in this world in regions where there is no autumn or winter cold).

In short, the shadows of Paradise, like all its blessings, are eternal. From this, it becomes clear that the gardens of Paradise have no autumn, and it also reveals that there is a radiance of sunlight or something similar in Paradise; otherwise, the term "shade" would have no meaning where there is no beam of light. As for what we read in Surah Al-Insan, verse 13: "لا يَرَوْنَ فِيها شَمْساً وَ لا زَمْهَرِيراً" (They will see therein neither [burning] sun nor [freezing] cold), it likely refers to the moderation of the weather, because the scorching heat of the sun and the severe cold are not present in Paradise, not that the sun does not shine at all.

The extinguishing of the sun's orb is also not evidence of its eternal disappearance, for the Quran says: On the Day of Resurrection, the earth and the heavens will be transformed into another earth and heavens (newer and vaster).

And if it is asked: "In a place where the sun has no scorching heat, what is the purpose of shade?"

Answer

We say that the grace of shade is not only to prevent the scorching heat of the sun; rather, the pleasant moisture evaporating from the leaves, combined with refreshing oxygen, gives a special delicacy and freshness to the shade. Therefore, the shade of a tree is never dry and lifeless like the shade of a room's ceiling.

At the end of the verse, after stating these three characteristics of Paradise, it says: "This is the end of those who are righteous, but the end of the disbelievers is the Fire!" (تِلْكَ عُقْبَى الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَ عُقْبَى الْكافِرِينَ النَّارُ).

In this beautiful expression, the blessings of Paradise are described with delicacy and in detail, but regarding the people of Hell, it states in a short, dry, and harsh sentence that the outcome of their affair is Hell!

Ayah

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ مَن يُنكِرُ بَعْضَهُۥ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَلَآ أُشْرِكَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا۟ وَإِلَيْهِ مَـَٔابِ (٣٦)
Those whom We have given the Book rejoice in what has been sent down to you. Some of the factions deny a part of it. Say, ‘Indeed I have been commanded to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him. To Him do I summon [all mankind] and to Him will be my return.’ (36)

Commentary

God-Worshippers and the Parties!

This verse refers to the differing reactions of people toward the revelation of the Quranic verses; how truth-seeking and right-demanding individuals were submissive and joyful regarding what was revealed to the Prophet, while the antagonistic and stubborn rose in opposition.

First, it says: "Those to whom We have given the Book rejoice at what has been revealed to you" (وَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ‌).

Given that the expression "آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ‌" (those to whom We gave the Book) and similar phrases throughout the Quran are usually applied to the Jews, Christians, and other followers of divine religions, there remains no doubt that here too it refers to them. That is, the truth-seekers among the Jews, Christians, and the like are happy and pleased with the revelation of these verses to you. This is because, on one hand, they see them as consistent with the signs they have in their possession, and on the other hand, these verses are a means of their liberation and salvation from the evil of superstitions and the pseudo-scholars of Judaism and Christianity who had enslaved them and deprived them of intellectual freedom and human perfection.

The view held by some great commentators that "الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ‌" refers to the companions and followers of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله seems very unlikely. This is because such an expression is not customary for Muslims; furthermore, it does not align well with the phrase "بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ‌" (at what has been revealed to you) [1]. The fact that Surah Ar-Ra'd is Meccan does not contradict what has been said, for although the main centers of the Jews were Medina and Khaybar, and the main center of the Christians was Najran and similar places, they undoubtedly traveled to Mecca. Their thoughts, culture, and beliefs were reflected in Mecca to some extent. For this reason, the people of Mecca, due to the signs these groups gave regarding the final Prophet of God, were awaiting the appearance of such a Prophet (the story of Waraqah ibn Nawfal and similar accounts are well-known).

In some other verses of the Quran, we also find evidence for this subject—that the true believers

[^1]: Because this would imply that "what has been revealed to you" is the same as "the Book," as both would refer to the Quran, whereas the context of contrast suggests that "the Book" refers to something other than "what has been revealed to you."

among the People of the Book were happy with the revelation of the Quranic verses to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In verse 52 of Surah Al-Qasas, we read: "Those to whom We gave the Book before it, they believe in it" (الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

Then it adds: "But among the parties (factions) are those who deny part of it" (وَ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ مَنْ يُنْكِرُ بَعْضَهُ‌).

This group refers to those among the Jews and Christians who were dominated by ethnic and religious prejudices and the like. For this reason, the Quran does not refer to them as "People of the Book," because they were not even submissive to their own divine books. Rather, they were in reality "parties" and factions that only followed their own partisan lines. This group denied whatever was not consistent with their tastes, desires, and prejudices.

It has also been suggested that "the parties" (al-Ahzab) refers to the polytheists, as they are mentioned with this same expression in Surah Al-Ahzab. They did not truly have a religion or creed; rather, they were scattered parties and groups that were united only by their opposition to the Quran and Islam.

The great commentator, the late Tabarsi, and some other commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas that the above verse refers to the idolaters' denial of God being described with the attribute "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Merciful). The People of the Book, especially the Jews, expressed joy at the presence of the word "Ar-Rahman" in the Quranic verses due to their familiarity with this description, while the polytheists of Mecca, who were unfamiliar with this attribute, would mock it.

At the end of the verse, it commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله not to pay attention to the opposition and stubbornness of this person or that, but rather to stand firm on his original path and the straight way: "Say, 'I am only commanded to worship Allah and not associate anything with Him. To Him I invite, and to Him is my return'" (قُلْ إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ وَ لا أُشْرِكَ بِهِ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا وَ إِلَيْهِ مَآبِ‌).

This is an indication that a true monotheist and a sincere worshipper of God has no path or program other than submission to all of Allah's commands. He is submissive to everything revealed by Allah and does not practice discrimination among them—accepting what suits his desires and denying what opposes them.

Key Point: Faith and Partisan Affiliations

In the above verse, we saw how Allah referred to the true believers among the Jews and Christians as "People of the Book," while referring to those who followed their prejudices and whims as "the parties." This is not limited to the history of early Islam or the Jews and Christians contemporary with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Always, the difference between true believers and those who merely claim faith is that true believers are purely submissive to the commands of Truth and do not make distinctions or practice discrimination among them. That is, they make their own desires and wishes subordinate to those commands, and only they are worthy of the titles "People of the Book" and "People of Faith."

However, those who are examples of "we believe in some and disbelieve in some" (نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَ نَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ‌)—meaning they accept whatever aligns with their line of thought, personal desires, and whims, and deny whatever does not; or they accept whatever is to their benefit and deny whatever goes against their personal interests—these are neither Muslims nor true believers. Rather, they are "parties" who seek their own objectives within religion, and therefore always practice discrimination regarding the teachings of Islam and its rulings.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا عَرَبِيًّا ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا وَاقٍ (٣٧)
Thus We have sent it down as a dispensation in Arabic; and should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you shall have against Allah neither any guardian nor defender. (37)

Certainly We have sent apostles before you, and We appointed for them wives and descendants; and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. There is a written [ordinance] for every term: (38)

Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes and with Him is the Mother Book. (39)
