Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], your duty is only to communicate, and it is for Us to do the reckoning. (40)

Commentary

"Definitive" and "Changeable" Events

These verses continue to follow the matters related to prophethood.

In the first verse, He says: "Just as We sent the Heavenly Book to the People of the Book and the previous prophets, We have also revealed this Quran to you, while it contains clear and manifest rulings" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ حُكْماً عَرَبِيًّا).

"Arabic" ('Arabiyyan), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means eloquent and clear speech (al-fasih al-bayyin min al-kalam). Therefore, when it is said "imra'atun 'arubah," it means a woman who is aware of her own chastity and purity. He then adds regarding the phrase حُكْماً عَرَبِيًّا: "It is said its meaning is 'eloquent,' which establishes the truth and nullifies falsehood."

The possibility has also been suggested that "Arabic" here means "noble," as this word appears with this meaning in the language as well.

Thus, the purpose of describing the Quran with this attribute is that its rulings are vivid and clear, leaving no room for exploitation or varied interpretations.

Consequently, following this expression in other verses, emphasis is placed on the issue of steadfastness and the absence of crookedness, or on knowledge and awareness. In Surah Az-Zumar, verse 28, we read: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوَجٍ ("An Arabic Quran, without any crookedness"). And in Surah Fussilat, verse 3, we read: كِتابٌ فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ ("A Book whose verses have been detailed, an Arabic Quran for a people who know"). In this way, the preceding and succeeding phrases in this verse confirm that the meaning of "Arabicity" is eloquence, clarity of expression, and being free from complexities.

This expression appears in seven surahs of the Quran, but in several instances, "لِسانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ" (a clear Arabic tongue) or similar phrases are mentioned, which may also carry the same meaning—namely, clarity of expression and absence of ambiguity.

Of course, in this specific case, it may also refer to the Arabic language, as Allah sent every prophet in the language of his own people so that he might first guide his own nation and then expand the scope of this revolution to other regions.

Then, with a threatening and decisive tone, He addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying:

"If you follow their desires after the truth has become clear to you, you shall be punished with Divine retribution, and no one will have the power to protect or shield you against Allah" (وَ لَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ بَعْدَ ما جاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَكَ مِنَ اللَّهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَ لا واقٍ).

Although the possibility of deviation certainly did not exist in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله given his station of infallibility ('ismah), gnosis, and awareness, this expression firstly clarifies that Allah has no private connection or so-called "kinship" with anyone. Even if the Prophet holds a lofty station, it is due to his submission, servitude, faith, and steadfastness. Secondly, it serves as an emphasis for others; for if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would have no immunity from Divine punishment should he deviate from the path of truth and incline toward falsehood, then the situation for others is clear. This is exactly like a person addressing his righteous child and saying: "If you make a mistake, I will punish you," so that others may take heed of their own accounts.

It is also necessary to mention that "Wali" (guardian/protector) and "Waq" (shielder) are similar in meaning, but they differ in that one expresses a positive aspect and the other a negative one; one means support and aid, while the other means defense and preservation.

The next verse is, in fact, a response to various objections raised by the enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

Among them: a group said, "Is it possible for a prophet to be of the human race, take wives, and have children?" The above verse answers them:

"This is not a new thing; We sent many messengers before you and assigned for them wives and children" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلًا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُمْ أَزْواجاً وَ ذُرِّيَّةً) [1]. Their objection shows that they are either ignorant of the history of the prophets or are feigning ignorance; otherwise, they would not have raised such an objection.

Another point is that they expect you to perform every miracle they suggest and whatever their whims dictate (whether they believe or not), but they must know: "No messenger can bring a miracle except by Allah's leave" (وَ ما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

The third objection was: why has the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله come and changed some rulings of the Torah or the Gospel? Are these not Heavenly Books revealed by Allah? Is it possible for Allah to abrogate His own command? (This objection is particularly consistent with what is known of the Jews, who believed in the impossibility of the abrogation of laws).

[1]: Some commentators have mentioned a "reason for revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for the above verse, stating it is a response to those who objected to the Prophet's multiple marriages, whereas Surah Ar-Ra'd is apparently Meccan, and polygamy was not an issue in Mecca.

The above verse answers them in its final phrase: "For every time, there is a prescribed decree and law" (until humanity reaches the stage of final maturity and the last command is issued) (لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ).

Therefore, it is not surprising that He reveals the Torah one day, the Gospel another, and then the Quran, because humanity, in its evolving and perfecting life, requires different and diverse programs.

There is also the possibility that the phrase لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ was a response to the objection of those who said: "If the Prophet is truthful, why does Divine punishment and torment not destroy his opponents?" The Quran answers them that everything has a time and is not without account; the time for punishment will also arrive in due course. [1]

The next verse serves as an emphasis and an argument for what was said at the end of the previous verse, which is that every event, every ruling, and every command has a specific time, as it is said: "Verily, matters are pledged to their times." And if you see some Heavenly Books taking the place of others, it is because "Allah effaces whatever He wills, just as He establishes matters according to His will and wisdom, and the Original Book—the Mother of the Book—is with Him" (يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ وَ عِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ).

Finally, as a further emphasis regarding the punishments the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله promised and which they were awaiting—even objecting as to why these promises were not being fulfilled—He says: "And whether We show you some of what We have promised them (of your victory, their defeat, the liberation of your followers, and the captivity of theirs during your lifetime) or We take you back to Us before these promises are fulfilled, in any case, your duty is only to convey the message, and the reckoning is upon Us" (وَ إِنْ ما نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ وَ عَلَيْنَا الْحِسابُ).

Please Note Two Important Points

1- The Tablet of Effacement and Establishment and the Mother of the Book

Although the phrase "يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ ..." in the above verses was revealed in the context of the descent of miracles or Heavenly Books to the prophets, it expresses a general and comprehensive law that has also been mentioned in various Islamic sources. That is:

The realization of beings and various events in the world has two stages: one is the stage of certainty, where no change can occur (referred to in the above verse as أُمُّ الْكِتابِ, the "Mother of the Book"), and the other is the non-definitive stage, or in other words, the "conditional" stage, where change can occur, and it is referred to as the stage of Effacement and Establishment (mahw wa ithbat).

Sometimes these two are referred to as the "Preserved Tablet" (Lawh Mahfuz) and the "Tablet of Effacement and Establishment." It is as if in one of these two tablets, what is written cannot be changed in any way and is completely preserved, while in the other, something may be written and then erased, and something else written in its place.

The reality of the matter is that sometimes we consider an event with its incomplete causes (asbab wa 'ilal naqisah). For example, we consider a lethal poison whose natural requirement is to destroy a human being, and we say anyone who drinks it will die, unaware that this poison also has an "antidote" which, if taken afterward, neutralizes its effect (of course, we might not be unaware, but we may not wish or the situation may not require us to speak of the antidote).

You observe here that this event—namely, "death due to drinking poison"—is not definitive; it belongs to the "Tablet of Effacement and Establishment," where change and transformation can occur in light of other events.

But if we consider the event with its complete cause ('illah tammah)—that is, the existence of the requirement, the gathering of all conditions, and the removal of all obstacles (in the above example, considering the poison along with the non-consumption of the antidote)—then the event is definitive. Its place is in the Preserved Tablet and the Mother of the Book, and no change can occur in it.

This can be stated in another way: Allah's knowledge has two stages: "knowledge of requirements and incomplete causes" and "knowledge of complete causes." That which pertains to the second stage is expressed as the Mother of the Book and the Preserved Tablet, and that which pertains to the first stage is expressed as the Tablet of Effacement and Establishment (otherwise, there is no physical tablet placed in a corner of the sky upon which things are written or erased).

From here, many questions arising from the study of Islamic sources are answered. For sometimes we read in narrations or in some verses of the Quran that a certain action leads to a certain effect and result, yet we sometimes do not see such a result.

This is because the realization of that result had conditions or obstacles; due to the absence of a condition or the presence of an obstacle, it did not manifest.

Also, many narrations regarding the Preserved Tablet, the Tablet of Effacement and Establishment, and the knowledge of the prophets and Imams are completely resolved with the above explanation. We provide a few examples below:

1- It is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that he asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله about the above verse. The Prophet said: "I shall delight your eyes with its interpretation, and I shall delight the eyes of my Ummah after me with its interpretation: Charity given correctly, kindness to parents, and performing good deeds turn misery into happiness, increase lifespan, and prevent tragic ends." [1] This indicates that happiness (sa'adah) and misery (shaqawah) are not inevitable and unavoidable matters. Even if a person has performed deeds that place him among the miserable, he can change his destiny by changing his stance, turning toward goodness, and especially by helping and serving Allah's creation, because the place for these matters is the Tablet of Effacement and Establishment, not the Mother of the Book.

It should be noted that what appears in the above hadith is a part of the verse's concept, presented as a clear example.

2- It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "Among matters are those that are definitive and will inevitably occur, and among matters are those that are suspended with Allah; He brings forward what He wills, effaces what He wills, and establishes what He wills..." [2] We also read from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام that he used to say: "Were it not for one verse in the Book of Allah, I would have told you of what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection!" The narrator says, "I asked, 'Which verse?' He said, 'Allah says: يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ وَ عِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ.'" [3] This hadith is evidence that at least a part of the knowledge of the great leaders of religion regarding various events pertains to the Tablet of Effacement and Establishment, while the Preserved Tablet with all its details is exclusive to Allah, and He teaches only the part He deems appropriate to His special servants.

In the supplications for the nights of the holy month of Ramadan, we also repeatedly read: "And if I am among the miserable, then efface me from the miserable and write me among the happy" (meaning: grant me the success to do this).

In any case, "Effacement and Establishment," in the manner described, has a comprehensive meaning that includes any change due to the alteration of conditions or the presence of obstacles. The fact that some commentators have pointed to a specific instance—for example, saying this phrase refers to the effacement of sins through repentance or the increase/decrease of sustenance due to changing conditions—does not seem correct unless the intention is to state one example.

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 11, p. 419. [2]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 11, p. 419. [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 512.

2- What is Bada'?

One of the controversial discussions that has arisen between the Shi'a and the Sunnis is the issue of "Bada'."

Fakhr al-Razi, in his commentary under the verse in question, says: "The Shi'a believe that Bada' is permissible for Allah, and the reality of Bada' according to them is that a person believes something, and then it appears that the reality is contrary to his belief, and they have clung to the verse يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ to prove this. Then Fakhr al-Razi adds: 'This belief is false, for Allah's knowledge is an essential attribute of His Essence, and that which is such, change and transformation in it are impossible.'"

Unfortunately, a lack of awareness regarding the Shi'a belief in the matter of Bada' has caused many Sunni brothers to attribute such false claims to the Shi'a. To clarify:

"Bada'" literally means "becoming manifest" and "complete clarity," and it has also been used to mean "regret," because a person who regrets something has certainly discovered something new.

Undoubtedly, Bada' in this sense is meaningless regarding Allah, and no sane or knowledgeable person could possibly imagine that something is hidden from Allah and then becomes manifest to Him over time. Fundamentally, such a statement is explicit and repulsive blasphemy (kufr), as it implies attributing ignorance to the Pure Essence of Allah and considering His Essence a locus for change and events. Far be it from the Imami Shi'a to hold such a possibility regarding the Holy Essence of Allah.

What the Shi'a believe regarding the meaning of Bada', and what they insist upon according to the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام: "He who does not know Allah through Bada' has not known Him with His true recognition," is this:

It often happens that, according to the outward appearance of causes and means, we feel that an event will occur, or the occurrence of such an event was told to one of the prophets, yet we later see that the event did not happen. At this moment, we say "Bada'" has occurred—meaning that what we saw as likely to happen according to outward appearances, and whose realization we considered certain, turned out otherwise.

The root and primary cause of this meaning is the same as discussed in the previous section: that sometimes our awareness is only of incomplete causes, and we do not see the conditions and obstacles, and we judge accordingly. Later, when we encounter the absence of a condition or the presence of an obstacle, and the opposite of what we predicted occurs, we become aware of these matters.

Similarly, sometimes a prophet or Imam becomes aware of the "Tablet of Effacement and Establishment," which is naturally subject to change and transformation, and sometimes it does not manifest due to encountering obstacles or the absence of conditions.

To clarify this reality, a comparison must be made between "Abrogation" (Naskh) and "Bada'": We know that the abrogation of rulings is permissible according to all Muslims—meaning a ruling may be revealed in the Sharia and people may imagine it to be permanent and eternal, but after a while, its abrogation is announced by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, and another ruling takes its place (as we have read in the story of the change of the Qibla in commentary, jurisprudence, and history).

This is, in fact, a type of "Bada'," but usually in legislative matters, laws, and rulings, it is called "Abrogation," while in matters of the creative order (takwini), it is called "Bada'."

For this reason, it is sometimes said: "Abrogation in rulings is a type of Bada', and Bada' in creative matters is a type of abrogation."

Can anyone deny such a logical matter? Except for one who does not distinguish between a complete cause and incomplete causes, or one who has been influenced by sinister anti-Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام propaganda and whose prejudices do not allow him to examine Shi'a beliefs in the books of the Shi'a themselves. It is strange that Fakhr al-Razi, despite bringing up the issue of Bada' regarding the Shi'a under the verse يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ ما يَشاءُ وَ يُثْبِتُ, did not notice that Bada' is nothing other than this "Effacement and Establishment," and with his characteristic prejudice, he harshly attacked the Shi'a for believing in Bada'.

Allow us to mention examples that everyone has accepted:

1- In the story of "Jonah" (Yunus), we read that the disobedience of his people caused Divine punishment to approach them. This great prophet, seeing them as unguided and deserving of torment, left them. But suddenly (Bada' occurred): one of the scholars of the people, seeing the signs of torment, gathered them and invited them to repentance. They all accepted, and the punishment whose signs had appeared was removed. (فَلَوْ لا كانَتْ قَرْيَةٌ آمَنَتْ فَنَفَعَها إِيمانُها إِلَّا قَوْمَ يُونُسَ لَمَّا آمَنُوا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُمْ عَذابَ الْخِزْيِ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ مَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى‌ حِينٍ) (Surah Yunus, verse 98).

2- It is also mentioned in Islamic histories that Prophet Jesus عليه السلام gave news of a bride who would die on her wedding night, but the bride remained safe contrary to Jesus's عليه السلام prediction! When they asked him about it, he said: "Did you give any charity in this path?" They said: "Yes." He said: "Charity repels definitive calamities!" [1]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, old edition, Vol. 2, p. 131, from Amali al-Saduq.

In reality, the pure soul of Jesus عليه السلام, through connection with the Tablet of Effacement and Establishment, gave news of the occurrence of such an event, while this event was conditional (conditioned on no obstacle like "charity" arising in its path). Since it encountered an obstacle, the result was something else.

3- In the story of Abraham, the idol-breaking hero, we read in the Quran that he was commanded to sacrifice Ishmael. Following this command, he took his son to the place of sacrifice, but when he showed his readiness, "Bada'" occurred, and it became clear that this command was a trial to test the level of obedience and submission of this great prophet and his son.

4- In the life of Moses عليه السلام, we also read that he was first commanded to leave his people for thirty days and go to the Divine meeting place to receive the rulings of the Torah, but later this period was extended by ten days (to test the Children of Israel).

Here the question arises: what is the benefit of these instances of Bada'?

The answer to this question, given what was mentioned above, is apparently not complex. For sometimes important matters such as testing a person or a nation, or the effect of repentance and returning to Allah (as in the story of Jonah), or the effect of charity and helping the needy and performing good deeds in removing painful events, and the like, require that the scene of future events be arranged one way first, and then another way with the change of conditions, so that people know their destiny is in their own hands. By changing their path and method, they are able to change their destiny, and this is the greatest benefit of Bada' (note carefully).

And if we read that one who has not known Allah through Bada' does not have complete recognition of Him, it refers to these very truths.

Thus, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, did not send any prophet except that He took from him three pledges: acknowledgment of servitude, the casting off of rivals [to Allah], and that Allah brings forward what He wills and delays what He wills." [1] In reality, the first pledge relates to obedience and submission to Allah, the second pledge relates to the struggle against polytheism, and the third pledge relates to the issue of Bada', the result of which is that man's destiny is in his own hands; by changing conditions, he can make himself subject to Allah's grace or His punishment.

The final word is that based on the above aspects, Shi'a scholars have said that when "Bada'" is attributed to Allah, it means "Ibda'"—that is, making manifest something that was not previously apparent and was not predicted.

As for attributing to the Shi'a the belief that Allah sometimes regrets His action or becomes aware of something He did not previously know, this is among the greatest crimes and most unforgivable slanders.

Therefore, it is narrated from the Imams that they said: "Whoever claims that something becomes manifest to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, today which He did not know yesterday, then disassociate from him." [2]

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 114; Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 61. [2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 61.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٤١)
Have they not seen how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Allah judges, and there is none who may repeal His judgement, and He is swift at reckoning. (41)
وَقَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَكْرُ جَمِيعًا ۖ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلْكُفَّـٰرُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٤٢)
Those who were before them [also] schemed; yet all devising belongs to Allah. He knows what every soul earns. Soon the faithless will know in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. (42)
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَسْتَ مُرْسَلًا ۚ قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (٤٣)
The faithless say, ‘You have not been sent [by Allah].’ Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you, and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book.’ (43)

Commentary

Humans and Societies Perish, and Allah Remains.

Since the previous verses were addressed to the deniers of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission, these verses continue that same discussion. The objective is to bring them to their senses through warnings, reasoning, and various methods, encouraging them to reflect and subsequently reform their condition.

First, It says: "Do these stubborn, arrogant people not see that We gradually reduce the earth from its outlying borders?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها).

It is clear that "earth" here refers to the "people of the earth." That is, do they not observe the reality that nations, civilizations, and governments are constantly in a state of decline and destruction? Nations that were stronger, more powerful, and more rebellious than them have all laid their faces beneath the dust. Even the scholars, greats, and learned men who were the pillars of the earth have closed their eyes to this world and joined eternity.

Is this general law of life—which applies to individuals, entire human societies, both small and large—not sufficient to awaken them, so they do not consider these few days of life as eternal and pass them in negligence?! Then He adds: "Judgment and command belong to Allah, and no one has the power to reject His decrees or prevent His command" (وَ اللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ).

"And He is swift in account" (وَ هُوَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ).

Therefore, on one hand, He has written the law of "annihilation" upon the foreheads of all individuals and nations; on the other hand, no one has the ability to change this decree or any of His other decrees; and thirdly, He settles the accounts of His servants with speed. Thus, His reward and punishment are certain.

In numerous narrations found in Tafsir al-Burhan, Nur al-Thaqalayn, and other commentaries and hadith sources, the above verse has been interpreted as the loss of scholars and thinkers, for their absence causes a deficiency in the earth and a shortage in human societies.

The great commentator Tabarsi, in interpreting this verse, narrates from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) as follows: "We diminish the earth through the passing away of its scholars, its jurists, and its best people" [^1]. In another hadith, we read that Abdullah ibn Umar recited this verse (أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها) when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), was martyred. He then said: "O Commander of the Faithful, you were the greatest side of knowledge; today the knowledge of Islam has diminished and the pillar of faith has passed" [^2]. Of course, as we mentioned, the verse has a broad meaning that includes every kind of deficiency, shortage, and the passing away of individuals and societies—and generally the people of the earth. It is a vivid and shaking warning to all people, whether good or bad, even to the scholars and thinkers who are the pillars of human societies, whose passing sometimes causes a whole world to diminish.

As for the possibility suggested by some commentators that the "diminishing of the earth" refers to the reduction of the lands of the disbelievers and the expansion of the lands of the Muslims, this does not seem correct considering that the Surah was revealed in Mecca. At that time, there were no conquests for the disbelievers to witness for the Quran to refer to.

Furthermore, the suggestion by some commentators immersed in natural sciences—that the verse refers to the earth shrinking at the poles and bulging at the equator—also seems very far-fetched, as the Quran in this verse is by no means in the position of stating such a thing.

In the next verse, the discussion continues, stating: It is not only this group who have risen to oppose you with plots and schemes, but "those before them also plotted and schemed" (وَ قَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

However, their plans were brought to naught and their plots were neutralized by Allah's command, for He is more aware of these matters than anyone. Rather, "all planning belongs to Allah" (فَلِلَّهِ الْمَكْرُ جَمِيعاً).

It is He who is aware of everyone's affairs and "He knows what every soul earns" (يَعْلَمُ ما تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ).

Then, with a threatening tone, He warns them of their end, saying: "The disbelievers will soon know for whom is the final home (the good or bad end in the hereafter)" (وَ سَيَعْلَمُ الْكُفَّارُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ).

In the final verse under discussion (just as this Surah began with the name of the Quran and the Book of Allah), Surah ar-Ra'd concludes with a stronger emphasis on the miraculous nature of the Quran, saying: "Those who disbelieve say, 'You are not a messenger'" (وَ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَسْتَ مُرْسَلًا).

Every day they devise an excuse; at every moment they demand a miracle; and in the end, they still say you are not a messenger! In response to them, say: "It is sufficient that two be witnesses between me and you: one is Allah, and the other is those who have knowledge of the Book and awareness of the Quran!" (قُلْ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتابِ).

Both Allah knows that I am His messenger, and those who have sufficient knowledge of this heavenly Book of mine—the Quran—also know well that this Book is not the product of a human brain, and it is impossible for it to have been revealed except from the Great God. This is a renewed emphasis on the inimitability (I'jaz) of the Quran from various aspects, which we have explained elsewhere, especially in the book The Quran and the Last Prophet.

Based on what we said above, the meaning of "مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتابِ" (he who has knowledge of the Book) refers to those aware of the contents of the Glorious Quran.

However, some commentators have suggested it refers to the scholars of the People of the Book—those who had read the signs of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) in their own scriptures and believed in him out of love and awareness.

But the first interpretation seems more correct.

In many narrations, it is stated that "مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتابِ" refers to Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) and the Imams of Guidance (عليهم السلام), which have been collected in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn and al-Burhan.

These narrations do not imply exclusivity; as we have said many times, they point to the most perfect instance or instances. In any case, they support the first interpretation we chose.

It is fitting to conclude this discussion with a narration from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Abu Sa'id al-Khudri says: I asked the Prophet about the verse "قالَ الَّذِي عِنْدَهُ عِلْمٌ مِنَ الْكِتابِ" (He who had knowledge of the Book—which appears in the story of Solomon). He said: "That was the executor (Wasi) of my brother Solomon ibn Dawud." I then asked about the verse "قُلْ كَفى‌ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ مَنْ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتابِ"—who is it speaking about and to whom does it refer? He said: "That is my brother Ali ibn Abi Talib" [^1]!

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 11, p. 427. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 301.

O Lord! Open the doors of Your mercy to us and grant us from the knowledge of Your Book. O Allah! Enlighten our hearts and empower our thoughts in the light of awareness of the Quran such that we attend to none but You, prioritize nothing over Your will, and do not become trapped in the straits of personal interests, narrow-mindedness, and egoism. May we not sow division among Your servants, nor lead our Islamic Revolution to the brink of danger, but rather prioritize the interests of Islam, the Quran, and the Muslim nation above all else.

O Allah! Awaken those who have imposed this devastating and destructive war—which the oppressive rulers of Iraq have forced upon us at the instigation of the enemies of Islam—from the sleep of negligence. If they are not to be awakened, then destroy them. And grant us that awareness in the light of Your Book so that we may utilize all legitimate and possible means to triumph over the enemies of truth and justice—Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

"End of Surah ar-Ra'd"
